A vine without fruit is worth nothing. Are we not planted in a cared-for garden, where we have all the tools needed to produce good deeds? Yet, there are many people who just pretend to believe or follow empty traditions instead of true faith. 

This vineyard was once beautiful but became neglected and ruined. This change came because God saw fit to discipline his people. Our lives go better when God blesses us rather than punishes us. Even the best parts of the church sometimes face tough times from God’s correction. People ask God to help the vine. Since he made it and owns it, he can take care of it with humble trust.

80.o: The Messiah, the Protector and Saviour of the church, is the Man of God's right hand; he is the Arm of the Lord, for all power is given to him. In him is our strength, by which we are enabled to persevere to the end. The vine, therefore, cannot be ruined, nor can any fruitful branch perish; but the unfruitful will be cut off and cast into the fire. The end of our redemption is, that we should serve Him who hath redeemed us, and not go back to our old sins.
80.m: The Messiah, who protects and saves the church, is called the Man of God’s right hand. He is the Lord’s strong helper because all power belongs to him. We get our strength from him, which helps us keep going until the end. So, the vine cannot be destroyed, and no good branches will die. But those that don’t produce fruit will be cut off and thrown into the fire. Our goal after being saved is to serve the one who freed us and not return to our old sins.

81.o: All the worship we can render to the Lord is beneath his excellences, and our obligations to him, especially in our redemption from sin and wrath. What God had done on Israel's behalf, was kept in remembrance by public solemnities. To make a deliverance appear more gracious, more glorious, it is good to observe all that makes the trouble we are delivered from appear more grievous. We ought never to forget the base and ruinous drudgery to which Satan, our oppressor, brought us. But when, in distress of conscience, we are led to cry for deliverance, the Lord answers our prayers, and sets us at liberty. Convictions of sin, and trials by affliction, prove his regard to his people. If the Jews, on their solemn feast-days, were thus to call to mind their redemption out of Egypt, much more ought we, on the Christian sabbath, to call to mind a more glorious redemption, wrought out for us by our Lord Jesus Christ, from worse bondage.
81.m:
All the worship we give to the Lord isn't enough for what he deserves, especially since he saved us from sin and punishment. 

God helped Israel many times, and they remembered these acts through special ceremonies. Making a rescue seem even better means showing how bad the problem was before. 

We should remember how low Satan made us go. When we feel guilty and ask God for help, he listens and frees us. Feeling sorry about sins and going through hard times show God cares for us.

If the Jews recalled being freed from slavery during their big holidays, then we Christians should think about an even greater freedom on Sundays. This freedom came because Jesus rescued us from a far worse kind of slavery.

81.o: We cannot look for too little from the creature, nor too much from the Creator. We may have enough from God, if we pray for it in faith. All the wickedness of the world is owing to man's wilfulness. People are not religious, because they will not be so. God is not the Author of their sin, he leaves them to the lusts of their own hearts, and the counsels of their own heads; if they do not well, the blame must be upon themselves. The Lord is unwilling that any should perish. What enemies sinners are to themselves! It is sin that makes our troubles long, and our salvation slow. Upon the same conditions of faith and obedience, do Christians hold those spiritual and eternal good things, which the pleasant fields and fertile hills of Canaan showed forth. Christ is the Bread of life; he is the Rock of salvation, and his promises are as honey to pious minds. But those who reject him as their Lord and Master, must also lose him as their Saviour and their reward.
81.m:
We shouldn't expect too much help from people or things around us, but we can always hope for plenty from God. If we ask for what we need with true belief, we might get enough. All the bad things in the world happen because people choose to act badly. People aren’t religious because they don’t want to be. God isn’t responsible for their sins—he lets them follow their own desires and decisions. 

God doesn’t want anyone to end up in trouble. Sinners often hurt themselves the most. Our problems last longer and our rescue takes more time because of sin. Just like the land of Canaan promised good things to faithful followers, Jesus offers spiritual blessings to believers who trust and obey him.

Jesus is the Bread of Life and the Rock of Salvation. His promises give comfort to those who love God. But if someone rejects Jesus as their leader, they miss out on being saved by him and receiving his rewards.

82.o: Magistrates are the mighty in authority for the public good. Magistrates are the ministers of God's providence, for keeping up order and peace, and particularly in punishing evil-doers, and protecting those that do well. Good princes and good judges, who mean well, are under Divine direction; and bad ones, who mean ill, are under Divine restraint. The authority of God is to be submitted to, in those governors whom his providence places over us. But when justice is turned from what is right, no good can be expected. The evil actions of public persons are public mischiefs.
82.m:
Leaders have power to help everyone. Leaders work for God by maintaining order and peace. They punish wrongdoers and protect good people. 

Good leaders follow God’s guidance. Bad leaders are kept in check by God too. We should obey the leaders God puts in charge. 

When leaders don’t act justly, nothing good happens. When important people do wrong, it causes harm to everyone.

82.o: It is hard for men to have honour put upon them, and not to be proud of it. But all the rulers of the earth shall die, and all their honour shall be laid in the dust. God governs the world. There is a righteous God to whom we may go, and on whom we may depend. This also has respect to the kingdom of the Messiah. Considering the state of affairs in the world, we have need to pray that the Lord Jesus would speedily rule over all nations, in truth, righteousness, and peace.
82.m:
It’s hard for people to handle praise without getting proud. But all leaders will eventually die, and their glory will fade away. God rules the world. We can trust in a just God who watches over us. 

This thought applies to Christ’s kingdom too. Given how things are in the world, we should pray that Jesus would soon reign over all nations with fairness, justice, and peace.

83.o: Sometimes God seems not to be concerned at the unjust treatment of his people. But then we may call upon him, as the psalmist here. All wicked people are God's enemies, especially wicked persecutors. The Lord's people are his hidden one; the world knows them not. He takes them under his special protection. Do the enemies of the church act with one consent to destroy it, and shall not the friends of the church be united? Wicked men wish that there might be no religion among mankind. They would gladly see all its restraints shaken off, and all that preach, profess, or practise it, cut off. This they would bring to pass if it were in their power. The enemies of God's church have always been many: this magnifies the power of the Lord in preserving to himself a church in the world.
83.m:
Sometimes it looks like God isn't paying attention when bad things happen to his people. But we can still ask for help from him, just like the psalmist did. 

All evil people are God’s enemies, especially those who hurt his followers. God protects his people secretly. The world doesn’t know about them. 

When the enemies of God’s church work together to destroy it, shouldn’t the friends of the church unite too?

Wicked people want there to be no religion left in the world. They wish everyone could live without rules and get rid of anyone who teaches, believes, or follows religious practices. If they had the power, they would do it.

There have always been lots of enemies against God’s church. This shows how powerful God is in keeping a church alive despite all the trouble.

83.o: All who oppose the kingdom of Christ may here read their doom. God is the same still that ever he was; the same to his people, and the same against his and their enemies. God would make their enemies like a wheel; unsettled in all their counsels and resolves. Not only let them be driven away as stubble, but burnt as stubble. And this will be the end of wicked men. Let them be made to fear thy name, and perhaps that will bring them to seek thy name. We should desire no confusion to our enemies and persecutors but what may forward their conversion. The stormy tempest of Divine vengeance will overtake them, unless they repent and seek the pardoning mercy of their offended Lord. God's triumphs over his enemies, clearly prove that he is, according to his name JEHOVAH, an almighty Being, who has all power and perfection in himself. May we fear his wrath, and yield ourselves to be his willing servants. And let us seek deliverance by the destruction of our fleshly lusts, which war against the soul.
83.m:
Anyone who opposes Christ’s kingdom can see what will happen to them. God hasn’t changed. He remains the same for his people and against those who fight him and his followers. 

God wants to make his enemies unstable, just like a spinning wheel. They won't have steady plans anymore. More than just being blown away like straw, they’ll be burned up too. This is what happens to evil people.

Making others fear your name might lead them to seek you out. When wishing bad things on our enemies, we should hope it leads them to turn to God instead. Unless they change their ways, God’s punishment will catch up to them. But if they ask for forgiveness, they can avoid trouble.

When God defeats his enemies, it shows he really is powerful, just like his name Jehovah suggests. He has all the strength and completeness needed. We should respect his anger and choose to serve him willingly.

We should try to free ourselves from harmful desires by letting God destroy them, since these urges fight against our souls.

84.o: The ordinances of God are the believer's solace in this evil world; in them he enjoys the presence of the living God: this causes him to regret his absence from them. They are to his soul as the nest to the bird. Yet they are only an earnest of the happiness of heaven; but how can men desire to enter that holy habitation, who complain of Divine ordinances as wearisome? Those are truly happy, who go forth, and go on in the exercise of religion, in the strength of the grace of Jesus Christ, from whom all our sufficiency is. The pilgrims to the heavenly city may have to pass through many a valley of weeping, and many a thirsty desert; but wells of salvation shall be opened for them, and consolations sent for their support. Those that press forward in their Christian course, shall find God add grace to their graces. And those who grow in grace, shall be perfect in glory.
84.m:
God’s rules give comfort to believers in this troubled world. Being near these rules means being close to God. This makes people miss them even more. These rules are like a safe home for the soul. 

But remember, following these rules is just a taste of the joy waiting in heaven. How can someone long to get there if they think God’s commands are boring?

True happiness comes from using Jesus’ power to keep practicing faith. On the journey to heaven, Christians might face hard times. But God will provide help and comfort along the way.

As people move forward in their faith, God gives them more grace. And those who continue growing in grace will reach full perfection in heaven.

84.o: In all our addresses to God, we must desire that he would look on Christ, his Anointed One, and accept us for his sake: we must look to Him with faith, and then God will with favour look upon the face of the Anointed: we, without him, dare not show our faces. The psalmist pleads love to God's ordinances. Let us account one day in God's courts better than a thousand spent elsewhere; and deem the meanest place in his service preferable to the highest earthly preferment. We are here in darkness, but if God be our God, he will be to us a Sun, to enlighten and enliven us, to guide and direct us. We are here in danger, but he will be to us a Shield, to secure us from the fiery darts that fly thick about us. Through he has not promised to give riches and dignities, he has promised to give grace and glory to all that seek them in his appointed way. And what is grace, but heaven begun below, in the knowledge, love, and service of God? What is glory, but the completion of this happiness, in being made like to him, and in fully enjoying him for ever? Let it be our care to walk uprightly, and then let us trust God to give us every thing that is good for us. If we cannot go to the house of the Lord, we may go by faith to the Lord of the house; in him we shall be happy, and may be easy. That man is really happy, whatever his outward circumstances may be, who trusts in the Lord of hosts, the God of Jacob.
84.m:
When we talk to God, we should ask him to see Jesus and accept us because of Jesus. We need to believe in Jesus first, and then God will look at us kindly. Without Jesus, we wouldn’t feel brave enough to approach God.

The writer of the psalm talks about loving God’s commands. We should think that spending one day in God’s presence is better than a thousand days anywhere else. Even the lowest position serving God is better than having a high position in the world.

We live in darkness now, but if God is our God, he will be like the sun for us—giving us light and life. We’re in danger too, but God will protect us from harmful attacks.

God hasn’t promised wealth and power, but he promises grace and glory to those who seek them through the right path. Grace means starting heaven on earth by knowing, loving, and serving God. Glory means finishing that happiness by becoming more like God and fully enjoying him forever.

Let’s focus on living honestly, and trust God to give us everything that’s good for us. If we can’t visit the house of the Lord, we can still reach out to the Lord of the house by faith. In him, we’ll find true happiness, even if our lives aren’t perfect.

Anyone who trusts in the Lord of hosts, the God of Jacob, is truly happy, no matter their situation.

85.o: The sense of present afflictions should not do away the remembrance of former mercies. The favour of God is the fountain of happiness to nations, as well as to particular persons. When God forgives sin, he covers it; and when he covers the sin of his people, he covers it all. See what the pardon of sin is. In compassion to us, when Christ our Intercessor has stood before thee, thou hast turned away thine anger. When we are reconciled to God, then, and not till then, we may expect the comfort of his being reconciled to us. He shows mercy to those to whom he grants salvation; for salvation is of mere mercy. The Lord's people may expect sharp and tedious afflictions when they commit sin; but when they return to him with humble prayer, he will make them again to rejoice in him.
85.m: We shouldn't forget past blessings just because we're facing troubles now. God’s favor brings happiness to both countries and individuals. When God forgives sins, he hides them completely. This is what forgiveness means. Out of love for us, God turns away from his anger when Jesus stands up for us. Only after we are forgiven by God can we enjoy peace with him. Salvation comes purely out of God’s kindness. His people might face hard and long trials if they sin, but when they pray humbly and turn back to God, he makes them happy again.

85.o: Sooner or later, God will speak peace to his people. If he do not command outward peace, yet he will suggest inward peace; speaking to their hearts by his Spirit. Peace is spoken only to those who turn from sin. All sin is folly, especially backsliding; it is the greatest folly to return to sin. Surely God's salvation is nigh, whatever our difficulties and distresses are. Also, his honour is secured, that glory may dwell in our land. And the truth of the promises is shown by the Divine mercy in sending the Redeemer. The Divine justice is now satisfied by the great atonement. Christ, the way, truth, and life, sprang out of the earth when he took our nature upon him, and Divine justice looked upon him well pleased and satisfied. For his sake all good things, especially his Holy Spirit, are given to those who ask him. Through Christ, the pardoned sinner becomes fruitful in good works, and by looking to and trusting in the Saviour's righteousness, finds his feet set in the way of his steps. Righteousness is a sure guide, both in meeting God, and in following him
85.m:
God will eventually give peace to his people. Even if there’s no peace around them, he’ll give them inner peace through his Spirit. But peace comes only to those who stop sinning. Sin is foolish, especially going back to old sins. It’s the worst thing you could do.

Surely, God’s salvation is near even when we face troubles. His honor is protected too, so that his glory stays in our land. This shows us that his promises are true because he sent the Savior. 

Divine justice is satisfied now because Jesus made full payment for our sins. When Jesus came to Earth, he was seen as pleasing and satisfying to God. Because of Jesus, all good things come to those who ask him, especially the Holy Spirit. 

Through Jesus, forgiven sinners become productive in doing good deeds. By looking to and trusting in Jesus’ righteousness, they find themselves walking on the right path. Righteousness guides us both in seeking God and following him.

86.o: Our poverty and wretchedness, when felt, powerfully plead in our behalf at the throne of grace. The best self-preservation is to commit ourselves to God's keeping. I am one whom thou favourest, hast set apart for thyself, and made partaker of sanctifying grace. It is a great encouragement to prayer, to feel that we have received the converting grace of God, have learned to trust in him, and to be his servants. We may expect comfort from God, when we keep up our communion with God. God's goodness appears in two things, in giving and forgiving. Whatever others do, let us call upon God, and commit our case to him; we shall not seek in vain.
86.m:
When we realize how poor and miserable we are, it strongly urges us to ask for help at God’s throne. The best way to take care of ourselves is by putting ourselves in God’s hands. 

I am someone you favor, who you have chosen and given the gift of holiness. Knowing that we’ve been changed by God’s grace gives us courage to pray. 

We can hope to find comfort from God if we stay connected to him. God shows his kindness in two ways—by giving us good things and by forgiving our sins. 

No matter what happens, we should turn to God and give our problems to him. We won’t search in vain if we do that.

86.o: Our God alone possesses almighty power and infinite love. Christ is the way and the truth. And the believing soul will be more desirous to be taught the way and the truth. And the believing soul will be more desirous to be taught the way and the truth of God, in order to walk therein, than to be delivered out of earthly distress. Those who set not the Lord before them, seek after believers' souls; but the compassion, mercy, and truth of God, will be their refuge and consolation. And those whose parents were the servants of the Lord, may urge this as a plea why he should hear and help them. In considering David's experience, and that of the believer, we must not lose sight of Him, who though he was rich, for our sakes became poor, that we through his poverty might be rich.
86.m:
God has all-powerful strength and endless love. Jesus is the path and the truth. People who believe will want to learn about God’s way and truth even more than they want relief from troubles on earth. 

Those who don’t put God first look for comfort elsewhere, but God’s kindness, mercy, and truth give us shelter and joy. 

If someone’s parents served the Lord, they can ask him for help because of that. 

When thinking about David’s story and a believer’s life, remember Jesus. He was rich but became poor so we could become rich through him.

87.o: Christ himself is the Foundation of the church, which God has laid. Holiness is the strength and firmness of the church. Let us not be ashamed of the church of Christ in its meanest condition, nor of those that belong to it, since such glorious things are spoken of it. Other foundation can no man lay than that is laid, even Jesus Christ. The glorious things spoken of Zion by the Spirit, were all typical of Christ, and his work and offices; of the gospel church, its privileges and members; of heaven, its glory and perfect happiness.
87.m:
Jesus Christ is the foundation of the church that God has set up. Holiness gives the church its strength and stability. We should not feel embarrassed about the church of Christ, even if it seems humble or poor. And we shouldn't be ashamed of those who belong to it because so many wonderful things are said about it.

No one can build on any other foundation except for Jesus Christ. All the beautiful things spoken about Zion by the Holy Spirit point to Christ, his role and duties. They also describe the gospel church, its benefits and members. And they talk about heaven, its beauty and complete joy.

87.o: The church of Christ is more glorious and excellent than the nations of the earth. In the records of heaven, the meanest of those who are born again stand registered. When God renders to every man according to his works, he shall observe who enjoyed the privileges of his sanctuary. To them much was given, and of them much will be required. Let those that dwell in Zion, mark this, and live up to their profession. Zion's songs shall be sung with joy and triumph. The springs of the joy of a carnal worldling are in wealth and pleasure; but of a gracious soul, in the word of God and prayer. All grace and consolation are derived from Christ, through his ordinances, to the souls of believers.
87.m:
The church of Christ is better than any nation on Earth. Even the humblest members of the church are recorded in heaven. 

When God judges people based on what they've done, He'll see who used the blessings of His house. Those who received much will be asked for much in return. 

People in Zion should remember this and live by their beliefs. They should sing joyful songs there. 

A worldly person finds happiness in money and fun, while someone with God’s grace finds joy in His Word and prayer. 

All the good things we receive come from Christ through His teachings and rituals, which bring comfort to believers.

88.o: The first words of the psalmist are the only words of comfort and support in this psalm. Thus greatly may good men be afflicted, and such dismal thoughts may they have about their afflictions, and such dark conclusion may they make about their end, through the power of melancholy and the weakness of faith. He complained most of God's displeasure. Even the children of God's love may sometimes think themselves children of wrath and no outward trouble can be so hard upon them as that. Probably the psalmist described his own case, yet he leads to Christ. Thus are we called to look unto Jesus, wounded and bruised for our iniquities. But the wrath of God poured the greatest bitterness into his cup. This weighed him down into darkness and the deep.
88.m: The first words of the psalmist are the only words of comfort in this psalm. Good people can suffer greatly, and they might have very sad thoughts about their troubles. They might even feel that their situation will get worse because of sadness and weak faith. The psalmist mostly felt upset by God’s displeasure. Even those who love God can sometimes feel like they are under His anger. Nothing hurts more than feeling God’s disapproval. Likely, the psalmist was describing his own feelings, but he points us toward Jesus. We should look at Jesus, who suffered from our sins. But God’s anger made his suffering much worse. It brought him great pain and despair.

88.o: Departed souls may declare God's faithfulness, justice, and lovingkindness; but deceased bodies can neither receive God's favours in comfort, nor return them in praise. The psalmist resolved to continue in prayer, and the more so, because deliverance did not come speedily. Though our prayers are not soon answered, yet we must not give over praying. The greater our troubles, the more earnest and serious we should be in prayer. Nothing grieves a child of God so much as losing sight of him; nor is there any thing he so much dreads as God's casting off his soul. If the sun be clouded, that darkens the earth; but if the sun should leave the earth, what a dungeon would it be! Even those designed for God's favours, may for a time suffer his terrors. See how deep those terrors wounded the psalmist. If friends are put far from us by providences, or death, we have reason to look upon it as affliction. Such was the calamitous state of a good man. But the pleas here used were peculiarly suited to Christ. And we are not to think that the holy Jesus suffered for us only at Gethsemane and on Calvary. His whole life was labour and sorrow; he was afflicted as never man was, from his youth up. He was prepared for that death of which he tasted through life. No man could share in the sufferings by which other men were to be redeemed. All forsook him, and fled. Oftentimes, blessed Jesus, do we forsake thee; but do not forsake us, O take not thy Holy Spirit from us.
88.m: Soul after death can tell about God’s faithfulness, justice, and kindness, but dead bodies cannot feel God’s comfort or praise Him. The psalmist decided to keep praying, even though help didn’t come quickly. We shouldn't stop praying just because answers don’t come right away. When trouble gets worse, we need to pray more seriously. Losing God’s presence hurts a believer deeply. If the sun leaves Earth, everything goes dark. People chosen for God’s blessings sometimes face His punishments too. This troubled the psalmist greatly. It’s hard when loved ones die or move away. A good person faces many trials. These arguments fit Jesus perfectly. He faced suffering all His life, starting young. Many people left Him. Sometimes we abandon Jesus too, but please don’t abandon us, Lord. Don’t take away Your Holy Spirit.

89.o: Though our expectations may be disappointed, yet God's promises are established in the heavens, in his eternal counsels; they are out of the reach of opposers in hell and earth. And faith in the boundless mercy and everlasting truth of God, may bring comfort even in the deepest trials.
89.m: Even if our hopes don't come true, God’s promises still stand firm. They are set in heaven, beyond what anyone on Earth or in hell can change. Trusting in God’s endless mercy and unchanging truth can give us comfort during the toughest times.

89.o: The more God's works are known, the more they are admired. And to praise the Lord, is to acknowledge him to be such a one that there is none like him. Surely then we should feel and express reverence when we worship God. But how little of this appears in our congregations, and how much cause have we to humble ourselves on this account! That almighty power which smote Egypt, will scatter the enemies of the church, while all who trust in God's mercy will rejoice in his name; for mercy and truth direct all he does. His counsels from eternity, and their consequences to eternity, are all justice and judgment.
89.m:
The more people know about God’s work, the more they admire it. Praise shows that God is unique and there is no one like Him. We should show respect when we worship God. But often, we don’t see enough reverence in our churches. This makes us feel sorry.

God’s great power that struck Egypt will defeat the church’s enemies. Those who trust in God’s mercy will be happy. Mercy and truth guide everything He does. From the start to the end of time, His plans are full of fairness and justice.

89.o: Happy are those who so know the joyful sound of the gospel as to obey it; who experience its power upon their hearts, and bring forth the fruit of it in their lives. Though believers are nothing in themselves, yet having all in Christ Jesus, they may rejoice in his name. May the Lord enable us to do so. The joy of the Lord is the strength of his people; whereas unbelief dispirits ourselves and discourages others. Though it steals upon us under a semblance of humility, yet it is the very essence of pride. Christ is the Holy One of Israel; and in him was that peculiar people more blessed than in any other blessing.
89.m: Those who truly understand and follow the good news feel happy because they let it change their lives. Even though they have nothing on their own, they find joy through what they have in Christ Jesus. Let the Lord help us do the same. God’s joy gives his people strength, while doubt weakens them and makes others lose hope too. Doubt might seem humble, but it’s really about pride. Christ is the Holy One of Israel, and he blesses his chosen people more than anyone else can.

89.o: The Lord anointed David with the holy oil, not only as an emblem of the graces and gifts he received, but as a type of Christ, the King Priest, and Prophet, anointed with the Holy Ghost without measure. David after his anointing, was persecuted, but none could gain advantage against him. Yet all this was a faint shadow of the Redeemer's sufferings, deliverance, glory, and authority, in whom alone these predictions and promises are fully brought to pass. He is the mighty God. This is the Redeemer appointed for us, who alone is able to complete the work of our salvation. Let us seek an interest in these blessings, by the witness of the Holy Spirit in our hearts. As the Lord corrected the posterity of David for their transgressions, so his people shall be corrected for their sins. Yet it is but a rod, not a sword; it is to correct, not to destroy. It is a rod in the hand of God, who is wise, and knows what he does; gracious, and will do what is best. It is a rod which they shall never feel, but when there is need. As the sun and moon remain in heaven, whatever changes there seem to be in them, and again appear in due season; so the covenant of grace made in Christ, whatever alteration seems to come to it, should not be questioned.
89.m:
The Lord anointed David with holy oil. This wasn't just a symbol of the good qualities and abilities he had. It also pointed to Jesus, who was both king, priest, and prophet. Jesus was filled with the Holy Spirit more than anyone else.

After David was anointed, he faced persecution, but nothing bad ever really happened to him. All of this was just a small preview of what the Savior would go through—his suffering, rescue, honor, and power. Only Jesus fulfilled those prophecies and promises completely.

Jesus is called the Mighty God. He’s the one chosen to save us. We should look for ways to connect with these blessings through the Holy Spirit within us.

Just as God disciplined David’s family when they did wrong, God will discipline his people when they sin. But it’s always done gently, not harshly. The punishment comes from God, who acts wisely and kindly, doing what’s best. People only face correction when needed.

Like the sun and moon stay in the sky even if they seem to change, the promise of God’s love in Christ remains true. Even if things seem different at times, we shouldn’t doubt it.

89.o: Sometimes it is not easy to reconcile God's providences with his promises, yet we are sure that God's works fulfil his word. When the great Anointed One, Christ himself, was upon the cross, God seemed to have cast him off, yet did not make void his covenant, for that was established for ever. The honour of the house of David was lost. Thrones and crowns are often laid in the dust; but there is a crown of glory reserved for Christ's spiritual seed, which fadeth not away. From all this complaint learn what work sin makes with families, noble families, with families in which religion has appeared. They plead with God for mercy. God's unchangeableness and faithfulness assure us that He will not cast off those whom he has chosen and covenanted with. They were reproached for serving him. The scoffers of the latter days, in like manner, reproach the footsteps of the Messiah when they ask, Where is the promise of his coming? 2Pe 3:3, 4. The records of the Lord's dealings with the family of David, show us his dealings with his church, and with believers. Their afflictions and distresses may be grievous, but he will not finally cast them off. Self-deceivers abuse this doctrine, and others by a careless walk bring themselves into darkness and distress; yet let the true believer rely on it for encouragement in the path of duty, and in bearing the cross. The psalm ends with praise, even after this sad complaint. Those who give God thanks for what he has done, may give him thanks for what he will do. God will follow those with his mercies, who follow him with praises.
89.m:
Sometimes it’s hard to see how God’s actions fit with his promises, but we know he always keeps his word. Even when Jesus was on the cross, it looked like God had abandoned him, but God didn’t break his covenant. The honor of King David’s family faded away. Crowns and thrones can lose their power, but Christ’s followers get a crown that lasts forever.

This teaches us how sin affects families, even noble ones where people serve God. They asked God for mercy. God’s love and faithfulness mean he won’t abandon those he chose and made promises to. People mocked them for following God. In the future, some will mock Christians by asking, “Where is the promise of his return?” (2 Peter 3:3–4).

God’s treatment of David’s family shows how he treats his church and believers. Their troubles might be tough, but God won’t leave them behind. Some misuse this truth, thinking they don’t need to obey God. Others live carelessly and face trouble. But true believers can trust this for comfort while doing right and carrying their crosses.

The song ends with praise, even after all these complaints. We can thank God now for what he’s done and later for what he’ll do. God follows those who praise him with more blessings.

90.o: It is supposed that this psalm refers to the sentence passed on Israel in the wilderness, Nu 14. The favour and protection of God are the only sure rest and comfort of the soul in this evil world. Christ Jesus is the refuge and dwelling-place to which we may repair. We are dying creatures, all our comforts in the world are dying comforts, but God is an ever-living God, and believers find him so. When God, by sickness, or other afflictions, turns men to destruction, he thereby calls men to return unto him to repent of their sins, and live a new life. A thousand years are nothing to God's eternity: between a minute and a million of years there is some proportion; between time and eternity there is none. All the events of a thousand years, whether past or to come, are more present to the Eternal Mind, than what was done in the last hour is to us. And in the resurrection, the body and soul shall both return and be united again. Time passes unobserved by us, as with men asleep; and when it is past, it is as nothing. It is a short and quickly-passing life, as the waters of a flood. Man does but flourish as the grass, which, when the winter of old age comes, will wither; but he may be mown down by disease or disaster.
90.m:
This psalm likely talks about the punishment given to Israel while they were in the desert, as mentioned in Numbers 14. 

The favor and protection from God give us true peace and comfort in this troubled world. Jesus Christ is our safe place where we can go for help. 

We humans are mortal, and everything we enjoy here is temporary too. But God lives forever, and those who believe in Him experience His eternal presence. 

When God uses illness or other troubles to bring people back to Himself, He wants them to turn away from sin and start living right. 

A thousand years means little to God’s endless existence. To Him, a moment and a million years feel similar. Everything that happens over a thousand years—past or future—is just as real to God as what happened an hour ago is to us. 

In the end, at the resurrection, our bodies and souls will come together again. 

Time moves quickly without us noticing, like someone sleeping through it. Once it’s gone, it’s gone, just like a brief flood. People might seem strong like grass, but when old age or trouble hits, they fade away. But we can still be cut down by sickness or disasters.

90.o: The afflictions of the saints often come from God's love; but the rebukes of sinners, and of believers for their sins, must be seen coming from the displeasure of God. Secret sins are known to God, and shall be reckoned for. See the folly of those who go about to cover their sins, for they cannot do so. Our years, when gone, can no more be recalled than the words that we have spoken. Our whole life is toilsome and troublesome; and perhaps, in the midst of the years we count upon, it is cut off. We are taught by all this to stand in awe. The angels that sinned know the power of God's anger; sinners in hell know it; but which of us can fully describe it? Few seriously consider it as they ought. Those who make a mock at sin, and make light of Christ, surely do not know the power of God's anger. Who among us can dwell with that devouring fire?
90.m:
The troubles that godly people face often come from God’s love. But when others punish them for their sins, it shows God’s displeasure. 

God knows our hidden sins and will hold us accountable for them. It’s foolish to try hiding our sins because we can’t hide them from God. 

Once our years are over, we can't get them back anymore than we can take back words we’ve said. Life is full of hard work and trouble. Sometimes it ends sooner than we expect. 

All these things teach us to fear God. Even fallen angels and people in hell know God’s anger. But none of us can truly describe its strength. Not many think about it enough. 

People who laugh at sin or Jesus don’t realize the power of God’s anger. Can anyone live through that burning fire?

90.o: Those who would learn true wisdom, must pray for Divine instruction, must beg to be taught by the Holy Spirit; and for comfort and joy in the returns of God's favour. They pray for the mercy of God, for they pretend not to plead any merit of their own. His favour would be a full fountain of future joys. It would be a sufficient balance to former griefs. Let the grace of God in us produce the light of good works. And let Divine consolations put gladness into our hearts, and a lustre upon our countenances. The work of our hands, establish thou it; and, in order to that, establish us in it. Instead of wasting our precious, fleeting days in pursuing fancies, which leave the possessors for ever poor, let us seek the forgiveness of sins, and an inheritance in heaven. Let us pray that the work of the Holy Spirit may appear in converting our hearts, and that the beauty of holiness may be seen in our conduct.
90.m:
People who want real wisdom should ask God for help. They need to pray and ask the Holy Spirit to teach them. 

They ask for God’s kindness because they know they don’t deserve anything. God’s favor will bring happiness and joy. It will also ease past sorrows.

Let God’s grace shine through our actions. When we feel God’s comfort, our hearts become joyful and our faces show it.

God, please strengthen what we do. To do this, first strengthen us. 

Instead of wasting time on empty dreams, let’s focus on asking for forgiveness and a place in heaven. 

We should pray for the Holy Spirit to change our hearts and make us holy in how we live.

91.o: He that by faith chooses God for his protector, shall find all in him that he needs or can desire. And those who have found the comfort of making the Lord their refuge, cannot but desire that others may do so. The spiritual life is protected by Divine grace from the temptations of Satan, which are as the snares of the fowler, and from the contagion of sin, which is a noisome pestilence. Great security is promised to believers in the midst of danger. Wisdom shall keep them from being afraid without cause, and faith shall keep them from being unduly afraid. Whatever is done, our heavenly Father's will is done; and we have no reason to fear. God's people shall see, not only God's promises fulfilled, but his threatenings. Then let sinners come unto the Lord upon his mercy-seat, through the Redeemer's name; and encourage others to trust in him also.
91.m:
Anyone who trusts in God for protection will find everything they need and want. People who have found comfort in turning to the Lord as their shelter naturally wish others could too. 

The spiritual life is guarded by God’s grace against Satan’s tricks, just like avoiding traps set by hunters. It’s also protected from harmful sins, which spread like a bad disease. 

Believers feel safe even in dangerous situations. Wisdom helps them avoid needless fears, while faith keeps them from being overly scared. 

Whatever happens, God’s will is done. We don’t need to be afraid. God’s followers will see both His promises kept and His warnings come true. 

Sinners should turn to the Lord through Jesus’ name, seeking mercy. They should also encourage others to trust in Him too.

91.o: Whatever happens, nothing shall hurt the believer; though trouble and affliction befal, it shall come, not for his hurt, but for good, though for the present it be not joyous but grievous. Those who rightly know God, will set their love upon him. They by prayer constantly call upon him. His promise is, that he will in due time deliver the believer out of trouble, and in the mean time be with him in trouble. The Lord will manage all his worldly concerns, and preserve his life on earth, so long as it shall be good for him. For encouragement in this he looks unto Jesus. He shall live long enough; till he has done the work he was sent into this world for, and is ready for heaven. Who would wish to live a day longer than God has some work to do, either by him or upon him? A man may die young, yet be satisfied with living. But a wicked man is not satisfied even with long life. At length the believer's conflict ends; he has done for ever with trouble, sin, and temptation.
91.m:
No matter what happens, nothing will harm the believer. Even if troubles come, they won't hurt them but bring good, even if it doesn’t feel joyful at first. People who truly know God love him. They pray often and ask for help. God promises to free believers from trouble eventually, and until then, he stays with them. 

God takes care of everything in a believer’s life. He keeps them safe here on Earth as long as it serves a purpose. To stay encouraged, believers look to Jesus. They live long enough to finish the tasks God gave them and get ready for heaven. Why would anyone want to live longer than God needs them to?

A person might die young and still be happy about their life. But someone evil isn’t happy even if they live a long time. Finally, the believer’s struggles end. They’re done forever with trouble, sin, and temptation.

92.o: It is a privilege that we are admitted to praise the Lord, and hope to be accepted in the morning, and every night; not only on sabbath days, but every day; not only in public, but in private, and in our families. Let us give thanks every morning for the mercies of the night, and every night for the mercies of the day; going out, and coming in, let us bless God. As He makes us glad, through the works of his providence for us, and of his grace in us, and both through the great work of redemption, let us hence be encouraged. As there are many who know not the designs of Providence, nor care to know them, those who through grace do so, have the more reason to be thankful. And if distant views of the great Deliverer so animated believers of old, how should we abound in love and praise!
92.m:
We get to praise the Lord every morning and evening, not just on Sundays, but every day. We can thank Him privately at home too. Each morning, let’s thank God for His kindness during the night. At night, let’s thank Him for what happened during the day. Whether leaving or returning, we should bless God.

As He brings joy through His care for us and His grace within us, including the gift of salvation, we should feel encouraged to praise Him. Many people don’t see God’s plans or don’t care about them. But those who do, because of God’s grace, have even more reasons to be grateful. Just as ancient believers were inspired by looking forward to their Savior, we should overflow with love and praise.

92.o: God sometimes grants prosperity to wicked men in displeasure; yet they flourish but for a moment. Let us seek for ourselves the salvation and grace of the gospel, that being daily anointed by the Holy Spirit, we may behold and share the Redeemer's glory. It is from his grace, by his word and Spirit, that believers receive all the virtue that keeps them alive, and makes them fruitful. Other trees, when old, leave off bearing, but in God's trees the strength of grace does not fail with the strength of nature. The last days of the saints are sometimes their best days, and their last work their best work: perseverance is sure evidence of sincerity. And may every sabbath, while it shows forth the Divine faithfulness, find our souls resting more and more upon the Lord our righteousness.
92.m:
Sometimes God gives success to bad people as a warning. But their good times don’t last long. 

We should look for God’s saving grace so we can see Jesus’ glory. Believers get their strength from God’s Holy Spirit through His Word. This helps keep them alive and growing strong.

Unlike regular trees that stop producing fruit when they’re old, God’s people stay strong even as they age. Often, the older Christians become, the better they serve God. Their lasting faith proves they are true followers.

May each Sunday remind us of God’s faithfulness and help us trust Him more and more.

94.o: We may with boldness appeal to God; for he is the almighty Judge by whom every man is judged. Let this encourage those who suffer wrong, to bear it with silence, committing themselves to Him who judges righteously. These prayers are prophecies, which speak terror to the sons of violence. There will come a day of reckoning for all the hard speeches which ungodly sinners have spoken against God, his truths, and ways, and people. It would hardly be believed, if we did not witness it, that millions of rational creatures should live, move, speak, hear, understand, and do what they purpose, yet act as if they believed that God would not punish the abuse of his gifts. As all knowledge is from God, no doubt he knows all the thoughts of the children of men, and knows that the imaginations of the thoughts of men's hearts are only evil, and that continually. Even in good thoughts there is a want of being fixed, which may be called vanity. It concerns us to keep a strict watch over our thoughts, because God takes particular notice of them. Thoughts are words to God.
94.m:
We can confidently turn to God because He is the powerful judge who decides everyone’s fate. This should give comfort to those who suffer injustice, encouraging them to endure quietly, trusting the one who judges fairly.

These prayers are like warnings to violent people. One day, God will hold accountable those who use harsh words against Him, His teachings, and His followers.

It’s hard to believe, unless you see it happen, that so many thinking beings would misuse their abilities without fearing God’s punishment.

Since all wisdom comes from God, He surely understands everything humans think. He sees that most thoughts are bad all the time.

Even good thoughts often lack firmness, which means they’re not always reliable.

We need to carefully guard our thoughts since God pays close attention to them. To God, thoughts are just like spoken words.

94.o: That man is blessed, who, under the chastening of the Lord, is taught his will and his truths, from his holy word, and by the Holy Spirit. He should see mercy through his sufferings. There is a rest remaining for the people of God after the days of their adversity, which shall not last always. He that sends the trouble, will send the rest. The psalmist found succour and relief only in the Lord, when all earthly friends failed. We are beholden, not only to God's power, but to his pity, for spiritual supports; and if we have been kept from falling into sin, or shrinking from our duty, we should give him the glory, and encourage our brethren. The psalmist had many troubled thoughts concerning the case he was in, concerning the course he should take, and what was likely to be the end of it. The indulgence of such contrivances and fears, adds to care and distrust, and renders our views more gloomy and confused. Good men sometimes have perplexed and distressed thoughts concerning God. But let them look to the great and precious promises of the gospel. The world's comforts give little delight to the soul, when hurried with melancholy thoughts; but God's comforts bring that peace and pleasure which the smiles of the world cannot give, and which the frowns of the world cannot take away. God is his people's Refuge, to whom they may flee, in whom they are safe, and may be secure. And he will reckon with the wicked. A man cannot be more miserable than his own wickedness will make him, if the Lord visit it upon him.
94.m:
A person is blessed when God corrects them and teaches them His ways through His Word and the Holy Spirit. They should see God’s mercy even during hard times. After suffering, there is rest waiting for God’s people. The one who causes trouble will also provide comfort later on.

The psalmist found help only from the Lord when everyone else let him down. We owe thanks not just to God’s strength, but also to His kindness for giving us support. If we’ve stayed strong in faith and duty, we should praise Him and cheer up others too.

The psalmist worried a lot about his situation, unsure what steps to take or what would happen next. Thinking too much about these things just makes you feel worse. Sometimes good people get troubled thoughts about God. 

But they should focus on God’s promises instead. Worldly pleasures don’t bring joy when you’re feeling sad. Only God’s blessings truly satisfy and can’t be taken away. God is a refuge where believers can find safety and security. One day, God will judge the wicked. People can’t be unhappier than their own sins make them.

95.o: Whenever we come into God's presence, we must come with thanksgiving. The Lord is to be praised; we do not want matter, it were well if we did not want a heart. How great is that God, whose the whole earth is, and the fulness thereof; who directs and disposes of all!, The Lord Jesus, whom we are here taught to praise, is a great God; the mighty God is one of his titles, and God over all, blessed for evermore. To him all power is given, both in heaven and earth. He is our God, and we should praise him. He is our Saviour, and the Author of our blessedness. The gospel church is his flock, Christ is the great and good Shepherd of believers; he sought them when lost, and brought them to his fold.
95.m:
When we go to meet with God, we need to come with thanks. We should praise the Lord because there is plenty to praise him about, even if sometimes we don't feel thankful enough. Think about how amazing God is—he owns everything on Earth and controls all things! 

The Lord Jesus, whom we learn to praise here, is a powerful God. One of his names is Mighty God, and another is God above all, forever blessed. He has all power in heaven and on Earth. He is our God, so we should praise him. He is our Savior, bringing us blessings. 

The Christian church is like his flock. Jesus is the Good Shepherd who cares for his people. He searches for those who are lost and brings them back to safety.

95.o: Christ calls upon his people to hear his voice. You call him Master, or Lord; then be his willing, obedient people. Hear the voice of his doctrine, of his law, and in both, of his Spirit: hear and heed; hear and yield. Christ's voice must be heard to-day. This day of opportunity will not last always; improve it while it is called to-day. Hearing the voice of Christ is the same with believing. Hardness of heart is at the bottom of all distrust of the Lord. The sins of others ought to be warnings to us not to tread in their steps. The murmurings of Israel were written for our admonition. God is not subject to such passions as we are; but he is very angry at sin and sinners. That certainly is evil, which deserves such a recompence; and his threatenings are as sure as his promises. Let us be aware of the evils of our hearts, which lead us to wander from the Lord. There is a rest ordained for believers, the rest of everlasting refreshment, begun in this life, and perfected in the life to come. This is the rest which God calls his rest.
95.m:
Christ asks his followers to listen to his message. When you call him Master or Lord, follow his teachings willingly. Listen to what he teaches about his doctrines, laws, and spirit. Listen carefully and obey.

Listen to Christ’s voice today because tomorrow may be too late. Make use of this time while it is still available. Listening to Christ means believing in him. People doubt the Lord mainly because of their stubborn hearts. We should learn from the mistakes of others so we don’t repeat them.

The complaints of ancient Israel serve as warnings to us. God doesn't get angry like humans do, but he gets upset by sin and those who commit it. Anything that causes God to punish someone surely shows its wickedness. His threats are just as reliable as his promises.

Be careful of the bad things in your heart that might cause you to turn away from God. Believers have been promised a place of peace and comfort. It starts here on earth and continues forever after death. This is the peaceful resting place that God has prepared.

96.o: When Christ finished his work on earth, and was received into his glory in heaven, the church began to sing a new song unto him, and to bless his name. His apostles and evangelists showed forth his salvation among the heathen, his wonders among all people. All the earth is here summoned to worship the Lord. We must worship him in the beauty of holiness, as God in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself. Glorious things are said of him, both as motives to praise and matter of praise.
96.m:
After Jesus completed his work on Earth and went to heaven, the church started singing a new song for him and praising his name. 

His followers told everyone about his saving power and amazing deeds. People everywhere were called to worship the Lord. 

We should honor him by showing our love for God’s holiness, seen through Jesus who brought peace between us and God. 

There are many wonderful things we can say about Jesus to inspire praise and thanksgiving.

96.o: We are to hope and pray for that time, when Christ shall reign in righteousness over all nations. He shall rule in the hearts of men, by the power of truth, and the Spirit of righteousness. His coming draws nigh; this King, this Judge standeth before the door, but he is not yet come. The Lord will accept the praises of all who seek to promote the kingdom of Christ. The sea can but roar, and how the trees of the wood can show that they rejoice we know not; but He that searches the heart knows what is the mind of the Spirit, and understands the words, the broken language of the weakest. Christ will come to judge the earth, to execute just vengeance on his enemies, and to fulfil his largest promises to his people. What then are we? Would that day be welcome to us? If this be not our case, let us now begin to prepare to meet our God, by seeking the pardon of our sins, and the renewal of our souls to holiness.
96.m:
We should hope and pray for the time when Christ will rule over all nations with justice. He will govern the hearts of people through the power of truth and the Holy Spirit. His return is near; the King and Judge stand at the door but have not arrived yet. The Lord accepts praise from those who work to spread Christ’s kingdom. 

The ocean may roar, and we do not know how trees express their joy, but God knows the thoughts and feelings of everyone. When Christ comes, he will judge the world, punish his enemies, and fulfill his promises to his followers. 

So, what about us? Will we welcome that day? If we are not ready, we need to start preparing now by asking for forgiveness of our sins and renewing ourselves to live holy lives.

97.o: Though many have been made happy in Christ, still there is room. And all have reason to rejoice in Christ's government. There is a depth in his counsels, which we must not pretend to fathom; but still righteousness and judgment are the habitation of his throne. Christ's government, though it might be matter of joy to all, will yet be matter of terror to some; but it is their own fault that it is so. The most resolute and daring opposition will be baffled at the presence of the Lord. And the Lord Jesus will ere long come, and put an end to idol worship of every kind.
97.m:
Even though many people have found happiness through Christ, there is still room for more. Everyone has reason to be glad about Christ’s rule. His plans are deep, and we cannot fully understand them. But his throne is based on fairness and justice.

Christ’s leadership brings joy to everyone who follows him. However, those who reject him will fear him. Even the bravest opponents will fail against the Lord. Soon, the Lord Jesus will come and stop all forms of false worship.

97.o: The faithful servants of God may well rejoice and be glad, because he is glorified; and whatever tends to his honour, is his people's pleasure. Care is taken for their safety. But something more is meant than their lives. The Lord will preserve the souls of his saints from sin, from apostacy, and despair, under their greatest trials. He will deliver them out of the hands of the wicked one, and preserve them safe to his heavenly kingdom. And those that rejoice in Christ Jesus, and in his exaltation, have fountains of joy prepared for them. Those that sow in tears, shall reap in joy. Gladness is sure to the upright in heart; the joy of the hypocrite is but for a moment. Sinners tremble, but saints rejoice at God's holiness. As he hates sin, yet freely loves the person of the repentant sinner who believes in Christ, he will make a final separation between the person he loves and the sin he hates, and sanctify his people wholly, body, soul, and spirit.
97.m:
God’s faithful followers can feel happy and joyful because God is honored. Anything that brings honor to him makes his people pleased.

Care is taken to keep them safe. This means more than just protecting their lives. 

The Lord keeps the souls of his holy ones safe from sin, falling away, and hopelessness during hard times. He saves them from evil forces and protects them until they reach heaven.

Those who find joy in Jesus and his glory have wells of happiness waiting for them. People who plant seeds with tears will harvest with joy. Upright-hearted people always get gladness. A hypocrite’s joy lasts only briefly.

Sinners fear, but true believers are glad about God’s holiness. Even though God hates sin, he still loves the person who turns from sin and trusts in Christ. He separates the loved person from hated sin, making his people completely holy—body, soul, and spirit.

98.o: A song of praise for redeeming love is a new song, a mystery hidden from ages and generations. Converts sing a new song, very different from what they had sung. If the grace of God put a new heart into our breasts, it will put a new song into our mouths. Let this new song be sung to the praise of God, in consideration of the wonders he has wrought. The Redeemer has overcome all difficulties in the way of our redemption, and was not discouraged by the services or sufferings appointed him. Let us praise him for the discoveries made to the world of the work of redemption; his salvation and his righteousness fulfilling the prophecies and promises of the Old Testament. In pursuance of this design, God raised up his Son Jesus to be not only a Light to lighten the Gentiles, but the glory of his people Israel. Surely it behoves us to inquire whether his holy arm hath gotten the victory in our hearts, over the power of Satan, unbelief, and sin? If this be our happy case, we shall exchange all light songs of vanity for songs of joy and thanksgiving; our lives will celebrate the Redeemer's praise.
98.m:
A song of praise for God’s saving love is a new song, a secret kept hidden until now. When someone becomes a believer, they start singing a new song, very different from before. If God gives us a new heart, he also gives us new words to sing. We should sing this new song to honor God because of the amazing things he has done.

The Savior overcame all the challenges on the path to save us. He didn’t give up even when faced with hard tasks or suffering. Praise him for showing the world how he fulfilled the old promises about saving us. God sent his Son Jesus to bring light to everyone, both Jews and Gentiles. 

We should ask if God’s strong arm has won the battle against evil in our hearts—against Satan, doubt, and sin. If so, we’ll stop singing empty songs and instead sing joyful, thankful ones. Our whole lives will show how much we praise the Redeemer.

98.o: Let all the children of men rejoice in the setting up the kingdom of Christ, for all may benefit by it. The different orders of rational creatures in the universe, seem to be described in figurative language in the reign of the great Messiah. The kingdom of Christ will be a blessing to the whole creation. We expect his second coming to begin his glorious reign. Then shall heaven and earth rejoice, and the joy of the redeemed shall be full. But sin and its dreadful effects will not be utterly done away, till the Lord come to judge the world in righteousness. Seeing then that we look for such things, let us give diligence that we may be found of him in peace, without spot, and blameless.
98.m: Let everyone be happy about the start of Christ’s kingdom because everyone can gain from it. In the time of the great Messiah, the various groups of thinking beings in the universe seem to be shown using figures of speech. Christ’s kingdom will bring blessings to everything around us. We wait for his return to start his wonderful rule. When he comes back, heaven and earth will celebrate, and those who are saved will have complete joy. However, sin and its bad results won’t end until the Lord returns to judge the world fairly. Since we hope for these things, let’s work hard to be at peace, clean, and free from blame when he arrives.

99.o: God governs the world by his providence, governs the church by his grace, and both by his Son. The inhabitants of the earth have cause to tremble, but the Redeemer still waits to be gracious. Let all who hear, take warning, and seek his mercy. The more we humble ourselves before God, the more we exalt him; and let us be thus reverent, for he is holy.
99.m: God controls the world through his care, guides the church with his kindness, and rules everything through his Son. People on Earth should be afraid, but the Savior is ready to show mercy. Everyone who hears this message should be warned and ask for God’s forgiveness. The more we humbly respect God, the more we honor him. So let’s be very respectful because he is holy.

99.o: The happiness of Israel is made out by referring to the most useful governors of that people. They in every thing made God's word and law their rule, knowing that they could not else expect that their prayers should be answered. They all wonderfully prevailed with God in prayer; miracles were wrought at their request. They pleaded for the people, and obtained answers of peace. Our Prophet and High Priest, of infinitely greater dignity than Moses, Aaron, or Samuel, has received and declared to us the will of the Father. Let us not only exalt the Lord with our lips, but give him the throne in our heart; and while we worship him upon his mercy-seat, let us never forget that he is holy.
99.m:
The happiness of Israel came from having good leaders who followed God’s word and laws. They knew that if they did this, God would answer their prayers. These leaders prayed effectively, and God worked miracles because of their requests. They spoke up for the people and got positive responses from God.

Our great Prophet and High Priest, more important even than Moses, Aaron, or Samuel, has told us what God wants. We should praise the Lord with our words and give him control over our hearts. As we pray on God’s mercy seat, we must remember that he is holy.

102.o: The whole word of God is of use to direct us in prayer; but here, is often elsewhere, the Holy Ghost has put words into our mouths. Here is a prayer put into the hands of the afflicted; let them present it to God. Even good men may be almost overwhelmed with afflictions. It is our duty and interest to pray; and it is comfort to an afflicted spirit to unburden itself, by a humble representation of its griefs. We must say, Blessed be the name of the Lord, who both gives and takes away. The psalmist looked upon himself as a dying man; My days are like a shadow.
102.m:
The entire Bible helps guide us in praying. But here, as in many places, the Holy Spirit gives us the right words to use. This prayer is given to those who are suffering. They should offer it to God. 

Even good people can feel completely overwhelmed by troubles. It’s important for us to pray. And it brings comfort to someone hurting to share their sorrows humbly. 

We need to say, “Blessed be the name of the Lord, who gives and takes away.” The psalmist saw himself as a person about to die. He said, “My days are like a shadow.”

102.o: We are dying creatures, but God is an everlasting God, the protector of his church; we may be confident that it will not be neglected. When we consider our own vileness, our darkness and deadness, and the manifold defects in our prayers, we have cause to fear that they will not be received in heaven; but we are here assured of the contrary, for we have an Advocate with the Father, and are under grace, not under the law. Redemption is the subject of praise in the Christian church; and that great work is described by the temporal deliverance and restoration of Israel. Look down upon us, Lord Jesus; and bring us into the glorious liberty of thy children, that we may bless and praise thy name.
102.m:
We are mortal beings, but God is eternal and protects his people. We can trust that he won't forget about us. 

When we think about how bad we are—our sins, our lack of faith, and all the mistakes in our prayers—we might worry that God won’t hear them. But we don’t need to fear, because we have someone who speaks up for us before God. And instead of being judged by the law, we are shown mercy.

The story of salvation gives Christians reasons to praise God. This big act of saving us is like bringing back the ancient nation of Israel from trouble.

Lord Jesus, look down on us and set us free to live as your children. Help us to give you thanks and praise.

102.o: Bodily distempers soon weaken our strength, then what can we expect but that our months should be cut off in the midst; and what should we do but provide accordingly? We must own God's hand in it; and must reconcile this to his love, for often those that have used their strength well, have it weakened; and those who, as we think, can very ill be spared, have their days shortened. It is very comfortable, in reference to all the changes and dangers of the church, to remember that Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, to-day, and for ever. And in reference to the death of our bodies, and the removal of friends, to remember that God is an everlasting God. Do not let us overlook the assurance this psalm contains of a happy end to all the believer's trials. Though all things are changing, dying, perishing, like a vesture folding up and hastening to decay, yet Jesus lives, and thus all is secure, for he hath said, Because I live ye shall live also.
102.m:
Health problems quickly wear us down, so it’s natural to wonder if our time will be cut short. When that happens, we need to prepare ourselves. We should recognize God’s role in these events and see them as part of His plan, even though He loves us. Sometimes people who use their strength wisely get weaker, and others who seem too valuable to lose have their days shortened. 

It’s comforting to know that Jesus Christ remains the same through all the changes and dangers facing the church. And when thinking about the end of our lives or losing loved ones, it helps to remember that God is eternal. Don’t forget the promise in this psalm that believers will eventually find happiness after all their trials. Even though everything around us is changing and dying, just like old clothes wearing out, Jesus is alive forever. Because He lives, you will live too.

103.o: By the pardon of sin, that is taken away which kept good things from us, and we are restored to the favor of God, who bestows good things on us. Think of the provocation; it was sin, and yet pardoned: how many the provocations, yet all pardoned! God is still forgiving, as we are still sinning and repenting. The body finds the melancholy consequences of Adam's offence, it is subject to many infirmities, and the soul also. Christ alone forgives all our sins; it is he alone who heals all our infirmities. And the person who finds his sin cured, has a well-grounded assurance that it is forgiven. When God, by the graces and comforts of his Spirit, recovers his people from their decays, and fills them with new life and joy, which is to them an earnest of eternal life and joy, they may then be said to return to the days of their youth, Job 33:25.
103.m:
When God forgives our sins, he removes what kept us from enjoying good things, and brings us back into his favor. He gives us blessings again. 

Think about how often we disappoint him—our sins are countless, yet God forgives them all. As long as we keep sinning and asking for forgiveness, God keeps forgiving us.

Our bodies suffer because of Adam’s mistake, getting sick and weak. Our souls do too. Only Jesus can forgive all our sins and heal all our problems.

If someone feels their sins have been healed, they can know for sure that those sins are truly forgiven. When God uses his grace and comfort to bring us back to health and fill us with joy, it’s like returning to our youthful strength. This joy is a sign of the eternal happiness to come, as Job 33:25 tells us.

103.o: Truly God is good to all: he is in a special manner good to Israel. He has revealed himself and his grace to them. By his ways we may understand his precepts, the ways he requires us to walk in; and his promises and purposes. He always has been full of compassion. How unlike are those to God, who take every occasion to chide, and never know when to cease! What would become of us, if God should deal so with us? The Scripture says a great deal of the mercy of God, and we all have experienced it. The father pities his children that are weak in knowledge, and teaches them; pities them when they are froward, and bears with them; pities them when they are sick, and comforts them; pities them when they are fallen, and helps them to rise; pities them when they have offended, and, upon their submission, forgives them; pities them when wronged, and rights them: thus the Lord pities those that fear him. See why he pities. He considers the frailty of our bodies, and the folly of our souls, how little we can do, how little we can bear; in all which his compassion appears.
103.m:
God is good to everyone, especially to Israel. He shows His kindness to them by revealing Himself and His grace. We learn about His rules and what He wants us to do from His actions. 

He has always been merciful. It’s different from people who find reasons to scold others without stopping. Can you imagine if God treated us that way?

The Bible talks a lot about God’s mercy, and we’ve all felt it. A father feels sorry for his children when they don’t know much, teach them, feel sorry when they’re stubborn, be patient with them, comfort them when they're sick, help them up when they fall, forgive them when they apologize after doing wrong, and stand up for them when they’re being mistreated. This is how the Lord feels sorry for those who respect Him.

Here’s why He feels sorry. He understands our weakness—both physically and mentally—and knows how little we can handle. All these show His compassion.

103.o: How short is man's life, and uncertain! The flower of the garden is commonly more choice, and will last the longer, for being sheltered by the garden-wall, and the gardener's care; but the flower of the field, to which life is here compared, is not only withering in itself, but exposed to the cold blasts, and liable to be cropt and trod on by the beasts of the field. Such is man. God considers this, and pities him; let him consider it himself. God's mercy is better than life, for it will outlive it. His righteousness, the truth of his promise, shall be unto children's children, who tread in the footsteps of their forefathers' piety. Then shall mercy be preserved to them.
103.m:
Life is very short and unpredictable. A flower in a garden usually lasts longer because it is protected and cared for. But a flower in a field wilts quickly and faces many dangers. 

This is true for people too. God understands our struggles and feels sorry for us. We should think about this ourselves. God’s mercy is even better than life itself because it continues after we die. 

His promises to be faithful to those who follow in their ancestors’ faith. This means mercy will keep going to future generations.

103.o: He who made all, rules all, and both by a word of power. He disposes all persons and things to his own glory. There is a world of holy angels who are ever praising him. Let all his works praise him. Such would have been our constant delight, if we had not been fallen creatures. Such it will in a measure become, if we are born of God. Such it will be for ever in heaven; nor can we be perfectly happy till we can take unwearied pleasure in perfect obedience to the will of our God. And let the feeling of each redeemed heart be, Bless the Lord, O my soul.
103.m:
God created everything and controls everything through his power. He makes sure everyone and everything brings honor to him. 

There are many good angels who always praise him. All of God’s creations should praise him too. 

If we hadn’t sinned, we would joyfully praise him all the time. When we’re changed by God, we start to do that more. In heaven, everyone will praise him without stopping. We won't truly be happy until we can obey God perfectly and enjoy doing so.

Each person saved by God should feel this way: bless the Lord, O my soul.

104.o: Every object we behold calls on us to bless and praise the Lord, who is great. His eternal power and Godhead are clearly shown by the things which he hath made. God is light, and in him is no darkness at all. The Lord Jesus, the Son of his love, is the Light of the world.
104.m: Everything we see should make us bless and praise the Lord, because he is so great. We can see his eternal power and divine nature in the things he has made. God is full of light and has no darkness in him. Jesus, the Son he loves, is the Light of the world.

104.o: When we reflect upon the provision made for all creatures, we should also notice the natural worship they render to God. Yet man, forgetful ungrateful man, enjoys the largest measure of his Creator's kindness. the earth, varying in different lands. Nor let us forget spiritual blessings; the fruitfulness of the church through grace, the bread of everlasting life, the cup of salvation, and the oil of gladness. Does God provide for the inferior creatures, and will he not be a refuge to his people?
104.m: When we think about how God takes care of all living things, we should also see how those creatures naturally praise him. But humans, often forgetful and unthankful, receive more kindness from their Creator than anyone else. The land changes in different places. We should also remember the spiritual gifts God gives, like the growth of the church because of God’s grace, the bread that gives eternal life, the cup that brings salvation, and the joy that comes from God. If God provides for animals, won’t he take care of his people?

104.o: We are to praise and magnify God for the constant succession of day and night. And see how those are like to the wild beasts, who wait for the twilight, and have fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness. Does God listen to the language of mere nature, even in ravenous creatures, and shall he not much more interpret favourably the language of grace in his own people, though weak and broken groanings which cannot be uttered? There is the work of every day, which is to be done in its day, which man must apply to every morning, and which he must continue in till evening; it will be time enough to rest when the night comes, in which no man can work. The psalmist wonders at the works of God. The works of art, the more closely they are looked upon, the more rough they appear; the works of nature appear more fine and exact. They are all made in wisdom, for they all answer the end they were designed to serve. Every spring is an emblem of the resurrection, when a new world rises, as it were, out of the ruins of the old one. But man alone lives beyond death. When the Lord takes away his breath, his soul enters on another state, and his body will be raised, either to glory or to misery. May the Lord send forth his Spirit, and new-create our souls to holiness.
104.m:
We should praise God for the steady cycle of day and night. Notice how some people act like animals, waiting only for the dark to do their evil deeds. Even if God listens to the cries of hungry animals, won’t He hear the prayers of His people, even if they are weak and barely audible?

Each day has tasks we need to complete during daylight. We must start fresh each morning and keep working until evening. Resting can come later when nighttime arrives, since there’s no work then.

The psalmist marvels at God’s creations. Man-made things often look imperfect up close, while natural things seem perfect and finely crafted. All of nature serves a purpose, showing God’s wisdom.
