If we haven’t learned to praise God, then we haven’t really understood anything. Our words should always follow God’s rules—neither saying things that are wrong nor staying silent when we should speak. The psalmist knew he couldn't do it alone and needed God’s help because only God could assist him. 

Religion was his chosen path. All believers hope for eternal salvation, so they ask God to guide them toward it. They want God’s laws and events to help them honor God. 

Sometimes he looked back at his past mistakes with regret. Yet, he prayed for God’s protection, knowing God paid a great price to save him. He asked God to find him and turn him around. When God searches, he always finds.

This psalm shows us how to examine ourselves. Are our hearts clean enough to make these prayers our own? Does God’s word shape our beliefs and actions? Do we use it to talk to Christ about our needs?

Those who live this way find true joy.

120.o: The psalmist was brought into great distress by a deceitful tongue. May every good man be delivered from lying lips. They forged false charges against him. In this distress, he sought God by fervent prayer. God can bridle their tongues. He obtained a gracious answer to this prayer. Surely sinners durst not act as they do, if they knew, and would be persuaded to think, what will be in the end thereof. The terrors of the Lord are his arrows; and his wrath is compared to burning coals of juniper, which have a fierce heat, and keep fire very long. This is the portion of the false tongue; for all that love and make a lie, shall have their portion in the lake that burns eternally.
120.m:
The psalmist faced much trouble because of people who lied about him. Let everyone who is good be saved from those who tell lies. They made up false stories about him. 

In his time of trouble, he prayed to God with strong feelings. God has power over people’s words. He got an answer to his prayers. 

Surely, bad people wouldn’t dare act the way they do if they understood the consequences. 

God’s anger is like sharp arrows. His fury is like hot coals from a bush that burn fiercely for a long time. 

This is the punishment for liars. Those who love to spread lies will get their share in the burning lake that lasts forever.

120.o: It is very grievous to a good man, to be cast into, and kept in the company of the wicked, from whom he hopes to be for ever separated. See here the character of a good man; he is for living peaceably with all men. And let us follow David as he prefigured Christ; in our distress let us cry unto the Lord, and he will hear us. Let us follow after peace and holiness, striving to overcome evil with good.
120.m:
It is very hard on a good person to be forced to stay around bad people, who they hope to avoid forever. We see what makes a good person—they try to get along with everyone. 

Like David, we should pray to God when we’re in trouble, just as Jesus did. He will listen to us. We should work towards peace and being holy, trying to defeat evil by doing good things instead.

122.o: The pleasure and profit from means of grace, should make us disregard trouble and fatigue in going to them; and we should quicken one another to what is good. We should desire our Christian friends, when they have any good work in hand, to call for us, and take us with them. With what readiness should we think of the heavenly Jerusalem! How cheerfully should we bear the cross and welcome death, in hopes of a crown of glory! Jerusalem is called the beautiful city. It was a type of the gospel church, which is compact together in holy love and Christian communion, so that it is all as one city. If all the disciples of Christ were of one mind, and kept the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace, their enemies would be deprived of their chief advantages against them. But Satan's maxim always has been, to divide that he may conquer; and few Christians are sufficiently aware of his designs.
122.m:
Going to church services should bring us joy and benefit, even if it means dealing with some trouble or tiredness along the way. We should encourage each other to do good things. When our Christian friends start a good project, we should ask them to include us. 

We should look forward to heaven with great hope. We should happily accept any suffering or even death because we believe in getting a reward there. The city of Jerusalem is described as beautiful. It represents the Christian church, where everyone works together in harmony, like one big family. 

If all of Jesus' followers agreed on everything and stayed united, their enemies couldn't hurt them as much. But Satan tries to cause division by making people disagree, and many Christians don’t realize this plan.

122.o: Those who can do nothing else for the peace of Jerusalem, may pray for it. Let us consider all who seek the glory of the Redeemer, as our brethren and fellow-travellers, without regarding differences which do not affect our eternal welfare. Blessed Spirit of peace and love, who didst dwell in the soul of the holy Jesus, descend into his church, and fill those who compose it with his heavenly tempers; cause bitter contentions to cease, and make us to be of one mind. Love of the brethren and love to God, ought to stir us up to seek to be like the Lord Jesus in fervent prayer and unwearied labour, for the salvation of men, and the Divine glory.
122.m:
People who cannot help Jerusalem in other ways should pray for it. We should see everyone who seeks the glory of the Savior as our brothers and sisters, even if we have some differences that don’t matter for our eternal life.

Holy Spirit of peace and love, who lived in the heart of Jesus, come down to his church and fill us with your gentle nature. Help end angry disagreements and make us think alike.

Love for each other and love for God should inspire us to pray hard and work tirelessly, just like Jesus did, to save people and honor God.

124.o: God suffers the enemies of his people sometimes to prevail very far against them, that his power may be seen the more in their deliverance. Happy the people whose God is Jehovah, a God all-sufficient. Besides applying this to any particular deliverance wrought in our days and the ancient times, we should have in our thoughts the great work of redemption by Jesus Christ, by which believers were rescued from Satan.
124.m: Sometimes God lets his people’s enemies win many battles so that he can show how powerful he is when he saves them. People who have God on their side are truly blessed because God can do anything. We should think about how God saved believers from Satan through Jesus Christ, just as we remember other times God helped his people in the past.

124.o: God is the Author of all our deliverances, and he must have the glory. The enemies lay snares for God's people, to bring them into sin and trouble, and to hold them there. Sometimes they seem to prevail; but in the Lord let us put our trust, and we shall not be put to confusion. The believer will ascribe all the honour of his salvation, to the power, mercy, and truth of God, and look back with wonder and thanksgiving on the way in which the Lord has led him. Let us rejoice that our help for the time to come is in him who made heaven and earth.
124.m: God should get the credit for saving us. Our enemies try to trap us in sin and problems. They sometimes seem to succeed. But if we trust in the Lord, we won’t be embarrassed. A true believer gives God all the praise for their rescue. They remember God’s power, mercy, and faithfulness with amazement and thanks. We can be happy knowing that God, who made everything, will always help us.

125.o: All those minds shall be truly stayed, that are stayed on God. They shall be as Mount Zion, firm as it is; a mountain supported by providence, much more as a holy mountain supported by promise. They cannot be removed from confidence in God. They abide for ever in that grace which is the earnest of their everlasting continuance in glory. Committing themselves to God, they shall be safe from their enemies. Even mountains may moulder and come to nothing, and rocks be removed, but God's covenant with his people cannot be broken, nor his care of them cease. Their troubles shall last no longer than their strength will bear them up under them. The rod of the wicked may come, may fall upon the righteous, upon their persons, their estates, their liberties, their families names, on any thing that falls to their lot; only it cannot reach their souls. And though it may come upon their lot, it shall not rest thereon. The Lord will make all work together for their good. The wicked shall only prove a correcting rod, not a destroying sword; even this rod shall not remain upon them, lest they distrust the promise, thinking God has cast them off.
125.m:
Those who trust in God will always stay strong. They will be steady like Mount Zion, which stands firm because God protects it. People who depend on God’s promises won’t lose faith. They will keep enjoying God’s grace forever. By putting their lives in God’s hands, they will be protected from their enemies. Mountains might wear down over time, and rocks can shift, but God’s promises to his people never break. Their trials will end before they become too hard to handle. 

The evil actions of others might affect the godly—like hitting them, taking away their possessions, or hurting their family—but these things can't touch their souls. When trouble comes, it won’t stick around long. God makes sure everything works out for their good. The punishment from sinners is just meant to correct, not destroy. This correction won’t continue if it causes doubt about God’s love and protection.

125.o: God's promises should quicken our prayers. The way of holiness is straight; there are no windings or shiftings in it. But the ways of sinners are crooked. They shift from one purpose to another, and turn hither and thither to deceive; but disappointment and misery shall befal them. Those who cleave to the ways of God, though they may have trouble in their way, their end shall be peace. The pleading of their Saviour for them, secures to them the upholding power and preserving grace of their God. Lord, number us with them, in time, and to eternity.
125.m:
God’s promises should encourage us to pray more. 

The path of holiness is straightforward; it doesn’t twist or turn. 

But sinners take crooked paths. They keep changing plans and trying to trick others. 

Disappointment and unhappiness will come to them. 

Those who follow God’s ways might face troubles along the way, but they will find peace in the end. 

Their Savior’s plea on their behalf ensures that God’s protective care will stay with them. 

Lord, count us among those people, both now and forever.

126.o: It is good to observe how God's deliverances of the church are for us, that we may rejoice in them. And how ought redemption from the wrath to come, from the power of sin and of Satan, to be valued! The sinner convinced of his guilt and danger, when by looking to a crucified Saviour he receives peace to his conscience, and power to break off his sins, often can scarcely believe that the prospect which opens to him is a reality.
126.m: It is good to notice how God helps His people, so we can celebrate those times. How valuable is being saved from God’s anger, from the control of sin and Satan? When someone realizes their wrongs and dangers, and looks to Jesus on the cross, they find peace and strength to stop sinning. Often, they can hardly believe what they see is real.

126.o: The beginnings of mercies encourage us to pray for the completion of them. And while we are in this world there will be matter for prayer, even when we are most furnished with matter for praise. Suffering saints are often in tears; they share the calamities of human life, and commonly have a greater share than others. But they sow in tears; they do the duty of an afflicted state. Weeping must not hinder sowing; we must get good from times of affliction. And they that sow, in the tears of godly sorrow, to the Spirit, shall of the Spirit reap life everlasting; and that will be a joyful harvest indeed. Blessed are those that mourn, for they shall be for ever comforted. When we mourn for our sins, or suffer for Christ's sake, we are sowing in tears, to reap in joy. And remember that God is not mocked; for whatever a man soweth that shall he reap, Ga 6:7–9. Here, O disciple of Jesus, behold an emblem of thy present labour and future reward; the day is coming when thou shalt reap in joy, plentiful shall be thy harvest, and great shall be thy joy in the Lord.
126.m:
The start of blessings encourages us to keep praying for more blessings. Even when we have plenty to thank God for, there’s always reason to pray. Saints who suffer often cry because they feel life’s troubles deeply. They face their hard times bravely by doing what they should. Crying shouldn’t stop them from working on God’s plan. Those who plant seeds of faith during tough times will one day see a wonderful harvest of happiness. People who grieve over sin or suffer for Christ will find comfort forever. 

When you’re sad about your mistakes or suffering for Jesus, know that you're planting seeds now that will grow into joy later. Remember, what you put in is what you’ll get out—just like in Galatians 6:7-9. Think of this, follower of Jesus: your work now means big rewards ahead. One day you'll enjoy a huge harvest of joy from the Lord.

129.o: The enemies of God's people have very barbarously endeavoured to wear out the saints of the Most High. But the church has been always graciously delivered. Christ has built his church upon a rock. And the Lord has many ways of disabling wicked men from doing the mischief they design against his church. The Lord is righteous in not suffering Israel to be ruined; he has promised to preserve a people to himself.
129.m: The enemies of God's people have tried very cruelly to destroy the faithful followers of God. But God has always saved his people. Jesus built his church on a strong foundation. And God stops evil people from hurting his church in many ways. It is right for God not to let Israel be destroyed because he promised to keep a people for himself.

129.o: While God's people shall flourish as the loaded palm-tree, or the green and fruitful olive, their enemies shall wither as the grass upon the house-tops, which in eastern countries are flat, and what grows there never ripens; so it is with the designs of God's enemies. No wise man will pray the Lord to bless these mowers or reapers. And when we remember how Jesus arose and reigns; how his people have been supported, like the burning but unconsumed bush, we shall not fear.
129.m: While God's people will thrive like well-grown palm trees or productive olive trees, their enemies will fade away like grass on rooftops, which never ripens because roofs in those places are flat. Wise people do not ask the Lord to bless those who harm others. When we think about how Jesus rose and rules, and how his followers have been protected just like the burning bush that was not consumed, we need not be afraid.

130.o: The only way of relief for a sin-entangled soul, is by applying to God alone. Many things present themselves as diversions, many things offer themselves as remedies, but the soul finds that the Lord alone can heal. And until men are sensible of the guilt of sin, and quit all to come at once to God, it is in vain for them to expect any relief. The Holy Ghost gives to such poor souls a fresh sense of their deep necessity, to stir them up in earnest applications, by the prayer of faith, by crying to God. And as they love their souls, as they are concerned for the glory of the Lord, they are not to be wanting in this duty. Why is it that these matters are so long uncertain with them? Is it not from sloth and despondency that they content themselves with common and customary applications to God? Then let us up and be doing; it must be done, and it is attended with safety. We are to humble ourselves before God, as guilty in his sight. Let us acknowledge our sinfulness; we cannot justify ourselves, or plead not guilty. It is our unspeakable comfort that there is forgiveness with him, for that is what we need. Jesus Christ is the great Ransom; he is ever an Advocate for us, and through him we hope to obtain forgiveness. There is forgiveness with thee, not that thou mayest be presumed upon, but that thou mayest be feared. The fear of God often is put for the whole worship of God. The only motive and encouragement for sinners is this, that there is forgiveness with the Lord.
130.m:
The only way to help a troubled soul is by turning to God alone. Many things seem like distractions or solutions, but only God can truly heal. Until people feel sorry for their sins and turn directly to God, they won't find any real help. The Holy Spirit helps those who are truly desperate by giving them a strong desire to pray sincerely and cry out to God. They should do this because they care about their souls and want to honor God.

Why do some people wait so long to seek help? Often it’s because they’re lazy or don’t believe they’ll get better. So let’s act now. We must humble ourselves before God and admit our wrongdoings. We can’t excuse ourselves or claim innocence. Our greatest comfort is knowing God forgives us, which is exactly what we need. Jesus Christ is our Savior and always defends us, helping us gain forgiveness.

God offers forgiveness, but this isn’t meant to encourage presumption. Instead, it leads us to fear and respect Him. Respecting God means worshipping Him properly. The main reason for sinners to seek God is that He provides forgiveness.

130.o: It is for the Lord that my soul waits, for the gifts of his grace, and the working of his power. We must hope for that only which he has promised in his word. Like those who wish to see the dawn, being very desirous that light would come long before day; but still more earnestly does a good man long for the tokens of God's favour, and the visits of his grace. Let all that devote themselves to the Lord, cheerfully stay themselves on him. This redemption is redemption from all sin. Jesus Christ saves his people from their sins, both from the condemning and from the commanding power of sin. It is plenteous redemption; there is an all-sufficient fulness in the Redeemer, enough for all, enough for each; therefore enough for me, says the believer. Redemption from sin includes redemption from all other evils, therefore it is a plenteous redemption, through the atoning blood of Jesus, who shall redeem his people from all their sins. All that wait on God for mercy and grace, are sure to have peace.
130.m:
I wait for the Lord to give me his gifts of grace and show his power. I should only hope for what he promises in his word. Just like someone eagerly waiting for sunrise, wanting light to come even before morning, a good person longs for signs of God’s favor and visits of his grace. Those who dedicate themselves to the Lord should trust in him patiently. 

Jesus saves his people from their sins—both from the guilt and control of sin. His salvation is plentiful; there is enough in the Redeemer for everyone, plenty for each one. So, the believer knows there is enough for them too. Being saved from sin means being saved from all other troubles, thanks to Jesus’ sacrifice. Anyone who waits for God’s mercy and help will surely find peace.

132.o: David bound himself to find a place for the Lord, for the ark, the token of God's presence. When work is to be done for the Lord, it is good to tie ourselves to a time. It is good in the morning to fix upon work for the day, with submission to Providence, for we know not what a day may bring forth. And we should first, and without delay, seek to have our own hearts made a habitation of God through the Spirit. He prays that God would take up his dwelling in the habitation he had built; that he would give grace to the ministers of the sanctuary to do their duty. David pleads that he was the anointed of the Lord, and this he pleads as a type of Christ, the great Anointed. We have no merit of our own to plead; but, for His sake, in whom there is a fulness of merit, let us find favour. And every true believer in Christ, is an anointed one, and has received from the Holy One the oil of true grace. The request is, that God would not turn away, but hear and answer their petitions for his Son's sake.
132.m:
David promised to find a place for the Lord, specifically for the ark, which showed where God was present. When doing work for the Lord, it’s best to set a specific time. In the morning, it’s wise to plan out the tasks for the day while being open to whatever might happen. 

First thing in the morning, we should ask God to make our hearts a home for him. David asked God to live in the house he built. He prayed that God would help those who serve in the temple do their jobs well. 

David said he was chosen by God, pointing ahead to Jesus, the greatest one chosen by God. We don’t earn anything on our own, but we can hope God will listen because of Jesus’ perfect life. Anyone who truly believes in Jesus is chosen by God and given the gift of God’s Spirit.

David asked God not to ignore them but to listen and answer their prayers because of Jesus.

132.o: The Lord never turns from us when we plead the covenant with his anointed Prophet, Priest, and King. How vast is the love of God to man, that he should speak thus concerning his church! It is his desire to dwell with us; yet how little do we desire to dwell with him! He abode in Zion till the sins of Israel caused him to give them up to the spoilers. Forsake us not, O God, and deliver us not in like manner, sinful though we are. God's people have a special blessing on common enjoyments, and that blessing puts peculiar sweetness into them. Zion's poor have reason to be content with a little of this world, because they have better things prepared for them. God will abundantly bless the nourishment of the new man, and satisfy the poor in spirit with the bread of life. He gives more than we ask, and when he gives salvation, he will give abundant joy. God would bring to nothing every design formed to destroy the house of David, until King Messiah should arise out of it, to sit upon the throne of his Father. In him all the promises centre. His enemies, who will not have him to reign over them, shall at the last day be clothed with shame and confusion for ever.
132.m:
The Lord never turns away from us when we ask for help based on the covenant with his chosen Prophet, Priest, and King. The love of God for his people is so great that he speaks about his church in such terms. God wants to stay close to us, but how often do we really want to be near him? 

He remained in Zion until the sins of Israel made him leave them to their enemies. Don't abandon us, God, and don’t let us suffer the same fate even if we are sinners. God’s people receive extra blessings on ordinary things, which makes those blessings especially sweet. Zion’s poor should be happy with what little they have in this world since they will get much better things later.

God will greatly bless the growth of the new person inside us and fill the spiritually humble with the true bread of life. He gives more than we ask for, and when he saves us, he fills us with great joy. God will stop anyone trying to harm the family of David until the King Messiah comes from that line to rule. All God’s promises come together through him.

Those who refuse to accept his rule will wear shame and confusion forever on the final day.

135.o: The subject-matter of praise, is the blessings of grace flowing from the everlasting love of God. The name of God as a covenant God and Father in Christ, blessing us with all spiritual blessings in him, is to be loved and praised. The Lord chose a people to himself, that they might be unto him for a name and a praise. If they do not praise him for this distinguishing favour, they are the most unworthy and ungrateful of all people.
135.m: We should praise God for his love and the blessings he gives us through Jesus. We honor God by loving him and thanking him for all the good things we receive because of Jesus. God chose a special group of people to belong to him so they could bring him glory. If these chosen people don’t thank him for this special favor, they are the worst kind of unthankful people.

135.o: God is, and will be always, the same to his church, a gracious, faithful, wonder-working God. And his church is, and will be, the same to him, a thankful, praising people: thus his name endures for ever. He will return in ways of mercy to them, and will delight to do them good.
135.m: God has always been and always will be a kind, faithful, and miracle-working God to his church. The church has always been and always will be a grateful, praising group to God. This keeps God’s name alive forever. He will show mercy to them and enjoy doing good things for them.

135.o: These verses arm believers against idolatry and all false worship, by showing what sort of gods the heathen worshipped. And the more deplorable the condition of the Gentile nations that worship idols, the more are we to be thankful that we know better. Let us pity, and pray for, and seek to benefit benighted heathens and deluded sinners. Let us endeavour to glorify his name, and recommend his truth, not only with our lips, but by holy lives, copying the example of Christ's goodness and truth.
135.m: These verses help believers avoid worshipping false gods. They show how the people who worship idols have very poor beliefs about their gods. The worse off those who follow false religions are, the more grateful we should be to know the truth. We should feel sorry for them, pray for them, and try to help them. We should honor God and share His truth through our words and actions. We should copy Jesus' kindness and honesty.

136.o: Forgetful as we are, things must be often repeated to us. By “mercy” we understand the Lord's disposition to save those whom sin has rendered miserable and vile, and all the provision he has made for the redemption of sinners by Jesus Christ. The counsels of this mercy have been from everlasting, and the effects of it will endure for ever, to all who are interested in it. The Lord continues equally ready to show mercy to all who seek for it, and this is the source of all our hope and comfort.
136.m:
We forget things quickly, so God needs to remind us often. When we think about "mercy," we mean God’s desire to help people who are suffering because of their sins. This includes everything God did through Jesus to save sinners. 

God planned this mercy before time began, and its benefits will last forever for everyone who receives it. God is always willing to show mercy to anyone who asks for it. That is where we get our hope and comfort.

136.o: The great things God did for Israel, when he brought them out of Egypt, were mercies which endured long to them; and our redemption by Christ, which was typified thereby, endures for ever. It is good to enter into the history of God's favours, and in each to observe, and own, that his mercy endureth for ever. He put them in possession of a good land; it was a figure of the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ.
136.m:
God did many wonderful things for Israel when he freed them from Egypt. These acts of kindness lasted a long time. Our salvation through Christ, which was like a picture of what happened then, lasts forever. 

It’s good to look at the story of God’s favors and see how His love continues. He gave them a nice land to live in. This was like a picture of the mercy we receive from Jesus.

136.o: God's everlasting mercy is here praised for the redemption of his church; in all his glories, and all his gifts. Blessed be God, who has provided and made known to us salvation through his Son. May we know and feel his redeeming power, that we may serve him in righteousness all our days. May He who giveth food to all flesh, feed our souls unto eternal life, and enliven our affections by his grace, that we may give thanks and praise to his holy name, for his mercy endureth for ever. Let us trace up all the favours we receive to this true source, and offer praise continually.
136.m:
We praise God for his endless love that brought salvation to his people through his Son. We thank him for providing and telling us about this salvation. May we see and feel God’s saving power so we can live righteously all our lives. 

May he who feeds everyone give us what we need for eternal life. And may his grace bring joy to our hearts so we can thank and praise his holy name forever. 

Let’s always remember where all our blessings come from and keep praising him.

137.o: Their enemies had carried the Jews captive from their own land. To complete their woes, they insulted over them; they required of them mirth and a song. This was very barbarous; also profane, for no songs would serve but the songs of Zion. Scoffers are not to be compiled with. They do not say, How shall we sing, when we are so much in sorrow? but, It is the Lord's song, therefore we dare not sing it among idolaters.
137.m: Their enemies had taken the Jews away from their homeland. To make things worse, these enemies mocked them. The enemies demanded that the Jews sing happy songs. This was very cruel and disrespectful. The Jews felt it was wrong to sing religious songs for such people who worship idols. Instead of asking, "How can we sing when we are so sad?" they said, "These are the Lord’s songs, so we cannot sing them here."

137.o: What we love, we love to think of. Those that rejoice in God, for his sake make Jerusalem their joy. They stedfastly resolved to keep up this affection. When suffering, we should recollect with godly sorrow our forfeited mercies, and our sins by which we lost them. If temporal advantages ever render a profession, the worst calamity has befallen him. Far be it from us to avenge ourselves; we will leave it to Him who has said, Vengeance is mine. Those that are glad at calamities, especially at the calamities of Jerusalem, shall not go unpunished. We cannot pray for promised success to the church of God without looking to, though we do not utter a prayer for, the ruin of her enemies. But let us call to mind to whose grace and finished salvation alone it is, that we have any hopes of being brought home to the heavenly Jerusalem.
137.m:
People tend to think about what they love. Those who are happy because of God often feel joyful about Jerusalem. They made a firm decision to keep those feelings alive.

When facing hardships, we should remember our past blessings with sadness over the sins that caused us to lose them. 

If someone uses good times to boast about themselves, then the worst thing has happened to them. We shouldn’t seek revenge; instead, we trust God’s promise that he will handle justice.

Those who are pleased by others’ troubles, especially Jerusalem’s troubles, will face consequences. 

We can ask for God’s church to succeed without wishing harm on its enemies. Instead, let’s focus on God’s grace and completed salvation, which gives us hope of reaching the heavenly Jerusalem.

138.o: When we can praise God with our whole heart, we need not be unwilling for the whole world to witness our gratitude and joy in him. Those who rely on his loving-kindness and truth through Jesus Christ, will ever find him faithful to his word. If he spared not his own Son, how shall he not with him freely give us all things? If God gives us strength in our souls, to bear the burdens, resist the temptations, and to do the duties of an afflicted state, if he strengthens us to keep hold of himself by faith, and to wait with patience for the event, we are bound to be thankful.
138.m: When we can praise God from our hearts, we shouldn’t mind others seeing our happiness and thankfulness. People who trust in God’s love and truth through Jesus will always find him true to his promises. Since God did not spare even his own son, how could he withhold anything else from us? If God gives us the strength to handle our troubles, resist temptation, and fulfill our duties, if he helps us stay faithful and patient, then we should be grateful.

138.o: Though the Lord is high, yet he has respect to every lowly, humbled sinner; but the proud and unbelieving will be banished far from his blissful presence. Divine consolations have enough in them to revive us, even when we walk in the midst of troubles. And God will save his own people that they may be revived by the Holy Spirit, the Giver of life and holiness. If we give to God the glory of his mercy, we may take to ourselves the comfort. This confidence will not do away, but quicken prayer. Whatever good there is in us, it is God works in us both to will and to do. The Lord will perfect the salvation of every true believer, and he will never forsake those whom he has created anew in Christ Jesus unto good works.
138.m:
Even though God is very high up, he still pays attention to humble sinners. But proud and doubtful people will be kept far away from him. 

God’s comfort is strong enough to lift our spirits, even when we face many problems. He saves his people so they can be filled with new life and goodness through the Holy Spirit.

If we praise God for his kindness, we can enjoy the peace it brings. Having faith makes us pray more, not less. 

Whatever good we do comes from God working inside us. He helps us want to do good things and then gives us the strength to follow through.

The Lord finishes what he starts in the lives of all true believers. He never leaves those he has made new in Christ Jesus to do good deeds.

139.o: God has perfect knowledge of us, and all our thoughts and actions are open before him. It is more profitable to meditate on Divine truths, applying them to our own cases, and with hearts lifted to God in prayer, than with a curious or disputing frame of mind. That God knows all things, is omniscient; that he is every where, is omnipresent; are truths acknowledged by all, yet they are seldom rightly believed in by mankind. God takes strict notice of every step we take, every right step and every by step. He knows what rule we walk by, what end we walk toward, what company we walk with. When I am withdrawn from all company, thou knowest what I have in my heart. There is not a vain word, not a good word, but thou knowest from what thought it came, and with what design it was uttered. Wherever we are, we are under the eye and hand of God. We cannot by searching find how God searches us out; nor do we know how we are known. Such thoughts should restrain us from sin.
139.m:
God knows everything about us, including all our thoughts and actions. It’s better to think deeply about God’s teachings, apply them to our lives, and pray sincerely than to just argue or be too curious. 

Everyone agrees that God knows everything and is everywhere, but people don’t always believe these facts truly matter. 

God watches every move we make—both the good ones and the bad ones. He sees which rules guide us, what goals we aim for, and who we choose to spend time with. Even when we’re alone, God knows what’s in our hearts. 

Every word we speak, whether empty or meaningful, comes from our thoughts and intentions, and God understands those completely. 

No matter where we go, God is watching over us. We can’t figure out how God knows so much about us, but we shouldn’t do anything wrong because we know he’s aware.

139.o: We cannot see God, but he can see us. The psalmist did not desire to go from the Lord. Whither can I go? In the most distant corners of the world, in heaven, or in hell, I cannot go out of thy reach. No veil can hide us from God; not the thickest darkness. No disguise can save any person or action from being seen in the true light by him. Secret haunts of sin are as open before God as the most open villanies. On the other hand, the believer cannot be removed from the supporting, comforting presence of his Almighty Friend. Should the persecutor take his life, his soul will the sooner ascend to heaven. The grave cannot separate his body from the love of his Saviour, who will raise it a glorious body. No outward circumstances can separate him from his Lord. While in the path of duty, he may be happy in any situation, by the exercise of faith, hope, and prayer.
139.m:
We can't see God, but he can see us. The psalmist didn’t want to leave the Lord. Where could I go? Even if I went to the farthest parts of the earth, to heaven, or even to hell, I couldn’t get away from God. Nothing can hide us from him—not even thick darkness. No disguise can keep anyone or anything hidden from God’s view. Places where people try to hide their sins are just as visible to God as openly wicked actions. 

On the other hand, believers can always stay close to their powerful friend. If someone tries to hurt them, their souls will quickly go to heaven. Death won’t separate their bodies from their Savior’s love. He will give them new, glorious bodies. 

No outside events can separate a believer from their Lord. As long as they follow God’s commands, they can find happiness anywhere through faith, hope, and prayer.

139.o: God's counsels concerning us and our welfare are deep, such as cannot be known. We cannot think how many mercies we have received from him. It would help to keep us in the fear of the Lord all the day long, if, when we wake in the morning, our first thoughts were of him: and how shall we admire and bless our God for his precious salvation, when we awake in the world of glory! Surely we ought not to use our members and senses, which are so curiously fashioned, as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin. But our immortal and rational souls are a still more noble work and gift of God. Yet if it were not for his precious thoughts of love to us, our reason and our living for ever would, through our sins, prove the occasion of our eternal misery. How should we then delight to meditate on God's love to sinners in Jesus Christ, the sum of which exceeds all reckoning! Sin is hated, and sinners lamented, by all who fear the Lord. Yet while we shun them we should pray for them; with God their conversion and salvation are possible. As the Lord knows us thoroughly, and we are strangers to ourselves, we should earnestly desire and pray to be searched and proved by his word and Spirit. if there be any wicked way in me, let me see it; and do thou root it out of me. The way of godliness is pleasing to God, and profitable to us; and will end in everlasting life. It is the good old way. All the saints desire to be kept and led in this way, that they may not miss it, turn out of it, or tire in it.
139.m:
God’s plans for us are very deep and hard to know. We get so many blessings from Him that we can’t even count them all. Thinking about God right when we wake up each morning could help us stay afraid of disappointing Him all day. And when we wake up in heaven, won't we praise God for saving us?

Our bodies are wonderfully made, but we shouldn't use them to do wrong things. Our minds and spirits are even more special gifts from God. Without His love, our smart brains might just lead us to hell because of our sins. So we should think often about how much God loves us through Jesus.

People who respect God hate sin and feel bad for those who do it. Still, we should pray for sinners too, since God can save anyone. Since He knows us better than we know ourselves, we should ask Him to show us our faults and fix them. 

Living a good life pleases God and helps us. It leads to eternal happiness. This is the best path to follow. Saints always want to stay on this path, not wander off or give up.

140.o: The more danger appears, the more earnest we should be in prayer to God. All are safe whom the Lord protects. If he be for us, who can be against us? We should especially watch and pray, that the Lord would hold up our goings in his ways, that our footsteps slip not. God is as able to keep his people from secret fraud as from open force; and the experience we have had of his power and care, in dangers of one kind, may encourage us to depend upon him in other dangers.
140.m:
When there is more danger, we should pray harder to God. Those who God protects are safe. If God is on our side, who can be against us?

We need to stay alert and pray that God will guide us so we don’t stumble. God can protect us from hidden tricks just as well as from obvious threats. Knowing how God has helped us before gives us confidence He will help us now.

140.o: Believers may pray that God would not grant the desires of the wicked, nor further their evil devices. False accusers will bring mischief upon themselves, even the burning coals of Divine vengeance. And surely the righteous shall dwell in God's presence, and give him thanks for evermore. This is true thanksgiving, even thanks-living: this use we should make of all our deliverances, we should serve God the more closely and cheerfully. Those who, though evil spoken of and ill-used by men, are righteous in the sight of God, being justified by the righteousness of Christ, which is imputed to them, and received by faith, as the effect of which, they live soberly and righteously; these give thanks to the Lord, for the righteousness whereby they are made righteous, and for every blessing of grace, and mercy of life.
140.m:
People who believe in God can ask Him not to let bad people get what they want. The troublemakers might end up hurting themselves because God will punish them. 

The good people will stay close to God and always be thankful. Being truly thankful means living a life where you show gratitude to God all the time. When we are saved from troubles, we should worship God more faithfully and happily.

Those who are treated badly by others but remain good in God’s eyes—because Jesus’ righteousness covers their sins—are the ones who thank God. They thank Him for making them righteous and for all the blessings in their lives.

141.o: Make haste unto me. Those that know how to value God's gracious presence, will be the more fervent in their prayers. When presented through the sacrifice and intercession of the Saviour, they will be as acceptable to God as the daily sacrifices and burnings of incense were of old. Prayer is a spiritual sacrifice, it is the offering up the soul and its best affections. Good men know the evil of tongue sins. When enemies are provoking, we are in danger of speaking unadvisedly. While we live in an evil world, and have such evil hearts, we have need to pray that we may neither be drawn nor driven to do any thing sinful. Sinners pretend to find dainties in sin; but those that consider how soon sin will turn into bitterness, will dread such dainties, and pray to God to take them out of their sight, and by his grace to turn their hearts against them. Good men pray against the sweets of sin.
141.m:
Make haste to me. People who understand how valuable God’s presence is will pray even more passionately. When our prayers come through Jesus, they become just as pleasing to God as the ancient sacrifices and offerings used to be. 

Prayer is like giving God our souls and deepest feelings. Wise people recognize the harm caused by using words carelessly. Even when others are mean to us, we might be tempted to speak without thinking. Living in a sinful world makes it important for good people to ask God to protect them from doing anything wrong.

Sin seems sweet to some, but wise people see how quickly it turns bitter. They ask God to help them stay away from sin and to keep it out of their lives. Good people pray to avoid the temptations of sin.

141.o: We should be ready to welcome the rebuke of our heavenly Father, and also the reproof of our brethren. It shall not break my head, if it may but help to break my heart: we must show that we take it kindly. Those who slighted the word of God before, will be glad of it when in affliction, for that opens the ear to instruction. When the world is bitter, the word is sweet. Let us lift our prayer unto God. Let us entreat him to rescue us from the snares of Satan, and of all the workers of iniquity. In language like this psalm, O Lord, would we entreat that our poor prayers should set forth our only hope, our only dependence on thee. Grant us thy grace, that we may be prepared for this employment, being clothed with thy righteousness, and having all the gifts of thy Spirit planted in our hearts.
141.m:
We should be ready to accept the correction from our heavenly Father and also from our brothers and sisters. A gentle rebuke won't hurt me if it helps soften my heart. People who once ignored God’s words will be happy to hear them during times of trouble because then their ears open up to learning. 

When life becomes hard, God’s Word feels sweet. We should pray to God. Ask Him to save us from Satan’s traps and those who do wrong. Using words like these, we ask you, Lord, to let our humble prayers express our only hope—our total trust in you. Give us your grace so we can prepare ourselves for this task. Dress us in your righteousness and fill our hearts with your Holy Spirit’s gifts.

143.o: We have no righteousness of our own to plead, therefore must plead God's righteousness, and the word of promise which he has freely given us, and caused us to hope in. David, before he prays for the removal of his trouble, prays for the pardon of his sin, and depends upon mercy alone for it. He bemoans the weight upon his mind from outward troubles. But he looks back, and remembers God's former appearance for his afflicted people, and for him in particular. He looks round, and notices the works of God. The more we consider the power of God, the less we shall fear the face or force of man. He looks up with earnest desires towards God and his favour. This is the best course we can take, when our spirits are overwhelmed. The believer will not forget, that in his best actions he is a sinner. Meditation and prayer will recover us from distresses; and then the mourning soul strives to return to the Lord as the infant stretches out its hands to the indulgent mother, and thirsts for his consolations as the parched ground for refreshing rain.
143.m:
We don't have any good deeds of our own to rely on, so we need to depend on God’s righteousness and the promises he made to us freely. Before praying to end his problems, David asks for forgiveness first and trusts only in God's mercy. 

He feels burdened by his worries and fears. But he thinks about how God helped his people in the past, including himself. He also considers God's great power. When we think about God's strength, we won’t be scared of what humans might do to us.

David looks up with strong hopes toward God. That’s the best thing to do when we feel overwhelmed. A true believer knows even their best actions still include sins. Thinking deeply about God and praying can help us deal with our troubles. And just like an eager child reaches out to its loving mother, a troubled soul longs to come back to God. It thirsts for God’s comfort like dry land yearns for life-giving rain.

143.o: David prays that God would be well pleased with him, and let him know that he was so. He pleads the wretchedness of his case, if God withdrew from him. But the night of distress and discouragement shall end in a morning of consolation and praise. He prays that he might be enlightened with the knowledge of God's will; and this is the first work of the Spirit. A good man does not ask the way in which is the most pleasant walking, but what is the right way. Not only show me what thy will is, but teach me how to do it. Those who have the Lord for their God, have his Spirit for their Guide; they are led by the Spirit. He prays that he might be enlivened to do God's will. But we should especially seek the destruction of our sins, our worst enemies, that we may be devotedly God's servants.
143.m:
David asks God to be happy with him and tell him that he is too. He tells God how sad he would be if God were far away. But David knows that hard times will pass and better days will come. 

He asks God to help him understand what God wants him to do. This is the first thing the Holy Spirit does. Good people don’t just want an easy path, they want to follow God’s plan. Show me what you want me to do, and then teach me how to do it. When someone has God as their God, they get the Holy Spirit to guide them. They walk by the Spirit’s guidance.

David also asks God to give him energy to do God’s will. The best thing we can do is destroy our sins, which are our biggest enemies. Then we can serve God wholeheartedly.

144.o: When men become eminent for things as to which they have had few advantages, they should be more deeply sensible that God has been their Teacher. Happy those to whom the Lord gives that noblest victory, conquest and dominion over their own spirits. A prayer for further mercy is fitly begun with a thanksgiving for former mercy. There was a special power of God, inclining the people of Israel to be subject to David; it was typical of the bringing souls into subjection to the Lord Jesus. Man's days have little substance, considering how many thoughts and cares of a never-dying soul are employed about a poor dying body. Man's life is as a shadow that passes away. In their highest earthly exaltation, believers will recollect how mean, sinful, and vile they are in themselves; thus they will be preserved from self-importance and presumption. God's time to help his people is, when they are sinking, and all other helps fail.
144.m:
When someone becomes famous even though they didn't have many advantages, they should remember that God taught them. It’s wonderful when God helps us control our emotions and desires.

We should start our prayers by thanking God for what he has already done for us. 

God gave the people of Israel a special desire to follow King David. This showed how God brings people under the rule of Jesus Christ.

People spend lots of time thinking about their bodies, but those bodies don’t last long. Life is short, like a quick shadow passing by.

Even when believers reach high positions, they still know they are weak and sinful on their own. This keeps them humble.

God often helps his people when everything else fails and they feel like they’re sinking.

144.o: Fresh favours call for fresh returns of thanks; we must praise God for the mercies we hope for by his promise, as well as those we have received by his providence. To be saved from the hurtful sword, or from wasting sickness, without deliverance from the dominion of sin and the wrath to come, is but a small advantage. The public prosperity David desired for his people, is stated. It adds much to the comfort and happiness of parents in this world, to see their children likely to do well. To see them as plants, not as weeds, not as thorns; to see them as plants growing, not withered and blasted; to see them likely to bring forth fruit unto God in their day; to see them in their youth growing strong in the Spirit. Plenty is to be desired, that we may be thankful to God, generous to our friends, and charitable to the poor; otherwise, what profit is it to have our garners full? Also, uninterrupted peace. War brings abundance of mischiefs, whether it be to attack others or to defend ourselves. And in proportion as we do not adhere to the worship and service of God, we cease to be a happy people. The subjects of the Saviour, the Son of David, share the blessings of his authority and victories, and are happy because they have the Lord for their God.
144.m:
New favors call for new thanks. We should thank God for the promises he makes as well as the good things he has already done. Being saved from danger or illness without being freed from sin and future punishment isn’t enough.

David wished for his people’s wellbeing. Parents are happier when they see their children doing well. They want to see their kids grow healthy and strong, bringing honor to God.

We should pray for plenty so we can give thanks, help our friends, and care for the poor. What use is having lots of food if we don't share?

Peace is important too. War causes many problems. When we stop following God, we lose our happiness.

Those who follow Jesus, the son of David, enjoy his protection and blessings. They are happy because God is their protector.

145.o: Those who, under troubles and temptations, abound in fervent prayer, shall in due season abound in grateful praise, which is the true language of holy joy. Especially we should speak of God's wondrous work of redemption, while we declare his greatness. For no deliverance of the Israelites, nor the punishment of sinners, so clearly proclaims the justice of God, as the cross of Christ exhibits it to the enlightened mind. It may be truly said of our Lord Jesus Christ, that his words are words of goodness and grace; his works are works of goodness and grace. He is full of compassion; hence he came into the world to save sinners. When on earth, he showed his compassion both to the bodies and souls of men, by healing the one, and making wise the other. He is of great mercy, a merciful High Priest, through whom God is merciful to sinners.
145.m:
When people pray a lot during hard times, they will later thank God for helping them. We should talk about God’s amazing plan to save us, especially when we tell others about his greatness. 

No event from the Old Testament shows God’s fairness better than what happened at the cross where Jesus died. His teachings were kind and loving, just like his actions. 

Jesus was very compassionate and came to Earth to help sinners. While here, he healed sick bodies and taught people wisdom. Because of his great mercy, he became a merciful priest who helps sinners get closer to God.

145.o: All God's works show forth his praises. He satisfies the desire of every living thing, except the unreasonable children of men, who are satisfied with nothing. He does good to all the children of men; his own people in a special manner. Many children of God, who have been ready to fall into sin, to fall into despair, have tasted his goodness in preventing their falls, or recovering them speedily by his graces and comforts. And with respect to all that are heavy laden under the burden of sin, if they come to Christ by faith, he will ease them, he will raise them. He is very ready to hear and answer the prayers of his people. He is present every where; but in a special way he is nigh to them, as he is not to others. He is in their hearts, and dwells there by faith, and they dwell in him. He is nigh to those that call upon him, to help them in all times of need. He will be nigh to them, that they may have what they ask, and find what they seek, if they call upon him in truth and sincerity. And having taught men to love his name and holy ways, he will save them from the destruction of the wicked. May we then love his name, and walk in his ways, while we desire that all flesh should bless his holy name for ever and ever.
145.m:
All of God's work shows his praise. He fulfills the needs of every living thing, except for humans who never seem satisfied. He does good things for everyone, especially his own people. 

Many of God’s children, who were about to fall into sin or feel hopeless, found his kindness in stopping them or quickly bringing them back through his grace and comfort. For anyone weighed down by sin, if they turn to Christ by faith, he will relieve their burdens and lift them up.

God listens closely to the prayers of his people. He is everywhere, but he is especially close to those who pray to him. He lives inside believers’ hearts through faith, and they live within him. When people call out to him, he is near to help them anytime they need it. 

If they sincerely ask for help, God will be close enough to give them what they want and find what they seek. After teaching people to love his name and follow his ways, he saves them from the fate of evil-doers. Let us then love his name and walk in his paths, hoping that all people will bless his holy name forever.

146.o: If it is our delight to praise the Lord while we live, we shall certainly praise him to all eternity. With this glorious prospect before us, how low do worldly pursuits seem! There is a Son of man in whom there is help, even him who is also the Son of God, who will not fail those that trust in him. But all other sons of men are like the man from whom they sprung, who, being in honour, did not abide. God has given the earth to the children of men, but there is great striving about it. Yet, after a while, no part of the earth will be their own, except that in which their dead bodies are laid. And when man returns to his earth, in that very day all his plans and designs vanish and are gone: what then comes of expectations from him?
146.m:
If we enjoy praising the Lord now, we will keep praising him forever. When we think about that wonderful future, how unimportant earthly things seem!

There is a Son of Man who can help us—Jesus Christ, the Son of God. He won't let down those who put their faith in him. But everyone else is just like Adam, who was once honored but didn’t stay that way.

God gave the world to humans, but people fight over it constantly. In the end, though, none of it belongs to them anymore than the ground where their bodies are buried.

When a person dies, all their plans disappear right away. So what good are hopes based on such fleeting dreams?

146.o: The psalmist encourages us to put confidence in God. We must hope in the providence of God for all we need as to this life, and in the grace of God for that which is to come. The God of heaven became a man that he might become our salvation. Though he died on the cross for our sins, and was laid in the grave, yet his thoughts of love to us did not perish; he rose again to fulfil them. When on earth, his miracles were examples of what he is still doing every day. He grants deliverance to captives bound in the chains of sin and Satan. He opens the eyes of the understanding. He feeds with the bread of life those who hunger for salvation; and he is the constant Friend of the poor in spirit, the helpless: with him poor sinners, that are as fatherless, find mercy; and his kingdom shall continue for ever. Then let sinners flee to him, and believers rejoice in him. And as the Lord shall reign for ever, let us stir up each other to praise his holy name.
146.m:
The psalmist tells us to trust God. We should rely on God’s care for everything we need now and his kindness for what comes next. God became human so he could save us. Even though he died on the cross and was buried, his love for us didn’t end. He rose from the dead to keep his promises. 

When Jesus lived on Earth, he showed by his miracles what he continues to do daily. He frees people trapped by their sins and Satan. He helps people see clearly. He gives spiritual food to those hungry for salvation. And he always helps the humble and needy. Poor sinners, feeling alone, receive mercy from him. His kingdom will last forever.

So let sinners run to him for help, and let believers be happy in him. As the Lord rules forever, let’s encourage each other to praise his holy name.

147.o: Praising God is work that is its own wages. It is comely; it becomes us as reasonable creatures, much more as people in covenant with God. He gathers outcast sinners by his grace, and will bring them into his holy habitation. To those whom God heals with the consolations of his Spirit, he speaks peace, assures them their sins are pardoned. And for this, let others praise him also. Man's knowledge is soon ended; but God's knowledge is a dept that can never be fathomed. And while he telleth the number of the stars, he condescends to hear the broken-hearted sinner. While he feeds the young ravens, he will not leave his praying people destitute. Clouds look dull and melancholy, yet without them we could have no rain, therefore no fruit. Thus afflictions look black and unpleasant; but from clouds of affliction come showers that make the soul to yield the peaceable fruits of righteousness. The psalmist delights not in things wherein sinners trust and glory; but a serious and suitable regard to God is, in his sight, of very great price. We are not to be in doubt between hope and fear, but to act under the gracious influences of hope and fear united.
147.m:
Praising God brings joy on its own. It suits us well as thinking beings, even better as those who have a special relationship with Him. He uses His grace to bring back lost sinners and welcomes them into His holy place. When God comforts someone with His Spirit, He tells them their sins are forgiven. For this reason, others should praise Him too.

Human understanding has limits, but God’s wisdom goes beyond measure. Even as He counts the stars, He listens to the prayers of brokenhearted sinners. Just as He cares for baby ravens, He won’t abandon His praying followers.

Clouds may seem gloomy, but without them there would be no rain and thus no crops. Similarly, troubles might look dark, but through these trials come blessings that lead to inner peace and goodness.

The writer finds no pleasure in what sinners boast about. Instead, showing respect and devotion to God holds great value in His eyes. We shouldn't hesitate between hope and fear. Rather, we should live guided by both hope and fear working together in harmony.

147.o: The church, like Jerusalem of old, built up and preserved by the wisdom, power, and goodness of God, is exhorted to praise him for all the benefits and blessings vouchsafed to her; and these are represented by his favours in the course of nature. The thawing word may represent the gospel of Christ, and the thawing wind the Spirit of Christ; for the Spirit is compared to the wind, Joh 3:8. Converting grace softens the heart that was hard frozen, and melts it into tears of repentance, and makes good reflections to flow, which before were chilled and stopped up. The change which the thaw makes is very evident, yet how it is done no one can say. Such is the change wrought in the conversion of a soul, when God's word and Spirit are sent to melt it and restore it to itself.
147.m:
The church, like ancient Jerusalem, is built up and protected by God’s wisdom, power, and kindness. It should thank Him for all the blessings He gives. These blessings are shown through His care in nature. 

Just as a warming word can thaw ice, so too can the gospel of Christ soften hearts. And just as a warming wind can melt snow, the Holy Spirit can bring about true change. Jesus said the Spirit is like the wind—no one knows where it comes from or goes.

When someone turns to God, their cold, stubborn heart becomes soft. They start crying tears of regret and thinking deeply about what they’ve done wrong. Just like melting ice shows clearly, the changes in a person’s life after turning to God are obvious. But no one understands exactly how God does it. When God sends His Word and Spirit, they work together to change people and help them become who they truly are meant to be.

148.o: We, in this dark and sinful world, know little of the heavenly world of light. But we know that there is above us a world of blessed angels. They are always praising God, therefore the psalmist shows his desire that God may be praised in the best manner; also we show that we have communion with spirits above, who are still praising him. The heavens, with all contained in them, declare the glory of God. They call on us, that both by word and deed, we glorify with them the Creator and Redeemer of the universe.
148.m:
In our dark and sinful world, we don't know much about the bright world above. We do know there is a world up there where happy angels praise God. 

The psalmist wants people to praise God in the best way possible. When we praise God, it shows we share something with those angels who are always praising him.

Everything in heaven tells us about God’s greatness. It calls on us to honor God with our words and actions, just like the angels do.

148.o: Even in this world, dark and bad as it is, God is praised. The powers of nature, be they ever so strong, so stormy, do what God appoints them, and no more. Those that rebel against God's word, show themselves to be more violent than even the stormy winds, yet they fulfil it. View the surface of the earth, mountains and all hills; from the barren tops of some, and the fruitful tops of others, we may fetch matter for praise. And assuredly creatures which have the powers of reason, ought to employ themselves in praising God. Let all manner of persons praise God. Those of every rank, high and low. Let us show that we are his saints by praising his name continually. He is not only our Creator, but our Redeemer; who made us a people near unto him. We may by “the Horn of his people” understand Christ, whom God has exalted to be a Prince and a Saviour, who is indeed the defence and the praise of all his saints, and will be so for ever. In redemption, that unspeakable glory is displayed, which forms the source of all our hopes and joys. May the Lord pardon us, and teach our hearts to love him more and praise him better.
148.m:
Even in this troubled world, God is still praised. Strong forces of nature, like storms, do exactly what God tells them to do. People who go against God’s words act even more violently than storms, but they still follow His plan.

Look at the land around you, including the mountains and hills. From both bare and fertile hilltops, there is plenty to inspire praise. Rational beings should use their minds to honor God.

Everyone should praise God—people of all kinds, rich and poor. Show that you belong to Him by praising His name constantly. God created us and saved us, making us close to Him. 

We can think of Jesus as "the Horn of His people"—He was raised up to save us, becoming our defense and praise forever. Through salvation, God shows great glory, giving us hope and joy. 

May the Lord forgive us and help our hearts to love and praise Him more.

149.o: New mercies continually demand new songs of praise, upon earth and in heaven. And the children of Zion have not only to bless the God who made them, but to rejoice in him, as having created them in Christ Jesus unto good works, and formed them saints as well as men. The Lord takes pleasure in his people; they should rejoice in Him. When the Lord has made sinners feel their wants and unworthiness, he will adorn them with the graces of his Spirit, and cause them to bear his image, and rejoice in his happiness for ever. Let his saints employ their waking hours upon their beds in songs of praise. Let them rejoice, even upon the bed of death, assured that they are going to eternal rest and glory.
149.m: New mercies call for new songs of praise, both on Earth and in heaven. The children of Zion need to thank God for making them and forming them into holy people through Christ. The Lord enjoys being with his people, so they should be happy with him too. After God helps sinners see their needs and faults, he fills them with his Spirit’s grace and makes them look like him. They can then enjoy his joy forever. His followers should sing praises even while lying awake at night. Even when dying, they can still be joyful, knowing they are heading to eternal peace and glory.

149.o: Some of God's servants of old were appointed to execute vengeance according to his word. They did not do it from personal revenge or earthly politics, but in obedience to God's command. And the honour intended for all the saints of God, consists in their triumphs over the enemies of their salvation. Christ never intended his gospel should be spread by fire and sword, or his righteousness by the wrath of man. But let the high praises of God be in our mouths, while we wield the sword of the word of God, with the shield of faith, in warfare with the world, the flesh, and the devil. The saints shall be more than conquerors over the enemies of their souls, through the blood of the Lamb and the word of his testimony. The completing of this will be in the judgement of the great day. Then shall the judgement be executed. Behold Jesus, and his gospel church, chiefly in her millennial state. He and his people rejoice in each other; by their prayers and efforts they work with him, while he goes forth in the chariots of salvation, conquering sinners by grace, or in chariots of vengeance, to destroy his enemies.
149.m:
Some of God's servants in the past were chosen to carry out punishment based on God’s instructions. They didn’t do it because of personal anger or political reasons, but because they followed God’s commands. 

The honor given to all of God’s followers comes from their victories over those who oppose their salvation. 

Christ never wanted his message to spread through violence or human anger. Instead, we should praise God highly while using the sword of God’s Word and the shield of faith to fight against the world, our sinful nature, and evil spirits. 

God’s people will overcome their enemies through the power of Jesus’ sacrifice and the truth of his teachings. This victory will be completed at the final judgment day. 

On that day, justice will be carried out. We see Jesus and his church, especially during the peaceful time after he returns. They celebrate together, and by praying and working hard, they help Jesus win over sinners with grace or punish those who resist him.
